proposals.title: A Computational and Experimental Framework for Whole-Body Photobiomodulation: Targeting Metastatic Cancer and Systemic Effects
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others demonstrates that photobiomodulation (PBM) can modulate tumor microenvironments, affect cancer stem cells (CSCs), and reduce metastasis in localized models. However, clinical translation for systemic diseases like metastatic cancer is limited by the inability to deliver therapeutic light doses to deep-seated or widely disseminated tissues. Current PBM devices are designed for superficial or small-area applications, lacking the engineering and dosimetry for effective whole-body or deep-organ treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-source, spectrally tuned whole-body light delivery system, guided by Monte Carlo simulation-optimized dosimetry, will achieve clinically significant fluence rates in deep tissues and induce systemic anti-metastatic and immunomodulatory effects not possible with localized treatment.
proposals.method: We will develop a human-sized, LED array-based whole-body irradiation chamber with independent control over near-infrared (810 nm) and red (660 nm) wavelength panels. A GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo simulation platform (using pre-2026 standards like MCML or a custom model in Python/Matlab) will model light propagation in a whole-body digital phantom to optimize source placement and power settings for target organs (e.g., liver, bone marrow). In vivo validation will use a murine model of breast cancer metastasis, with experimental groups receiving whole-body PBM, localized PBM, or no light, combined with or without curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles to assess synergistic effects on metastatic burden and CSC populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated computational-experimental framework for whole-body PBM dosimetry and delivery. It will generate critical preclinical data on the systemic biological effects of large-field PBM, specifically on metastasis and immune modulation, moving the field beyond localized wound healing and pain applications. The proposed system design and validation protocol will establish a new methodology for scaling PBM to treat human systemic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While Monte Carlo models exist for light transport, their application to optimize a multi-wavelength, whole-body delivery system for systemic oncologic effects is novel. Combining this engineering approach with Hamblin's established research on PBM, nanoparticles, and metastasis creates a unique strategy to overcome the fundamental depth limitation of light-based therapies.

proposals.title: Targeting Cancer Stem Cells and Metastasis with Blue and Green Wavelength Photobiomodulation Combined with Nitric Oxide-Releasing Nanoparticles
proposals.motivation: Photobiomodulation (PBM) for cancer treatment has been largely confined to red/NIR wavelengths due to their tissue penetration and known effects on cytochrome c oxidase. However, pre-2026 work shows shorter wavelengths (blue/green) can generate distinct reactive oxygen species (ROS) profiles and influence other cellular chromophores like flavoproteins. This is significant because cancer stem cells (CSCs) and metastatic processes have unique metabolic and redox vulnerabilities that may be differentially targeted by these wavelengths, offering a new therapeutic dimension.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that blue (450 nm) and green (525 nm) light PBM, when combined with a nanoparticle-delivered nitric oxide (NO) photosensitizer (like a nitro ruthenium complex), will selectively impair CSC viability and metastatic potential in vitro and in vivo by generating wavelength-specific ROS/RNS cocktails that disrupt CSC metabolism and migration signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize curcumin-loaded, NO-releasing iron oxide nanoparticles (similar to our prior diabetic wound work) functionalized with a CSC-targeting ligand (e.g., CD44 antibody). Using in vitro models of breast cancer CSCs (mammospheres), we will conduct a controlled study with experimental groups receiving: 1) no treatment (control), 2) nanoparticles alone, 3) blue light alone, 4) green light alone, 5) nanoparticles + blue light, 6) nanoparticles + green light. Outcomes will include mammosphere formation assay, ALDH activity, metastatic markers (MMP-9, vimentin), and intracellular ROS/RNS flux measured via specific fluorescent probes. A subsequent in vivo study in an orthotopic, metastatic mouse model will assess primary tumor growth and lung metastasis using bioluminescence imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel combinatorial PBM paradigm that moves beyond red/NIR exclusivity, providing a mechanistic rationale for using shorter wavelengths in oncology. It will deliver a targeted, light-activated nanoplatform designed to exploit the distinct photochemistry of blue/green light to attack CSCs and metastasis, potentially improving outcomes over single-modality treatments.
proposals.novelty: While blue light has been studied for antibacterial effects and surface applications, its systematic use in combination with NO-releasing nanoparticles to selectively target the deep-seated, therapy-resistant CSC population is a novel and non-obvious application of PBM, merging insights from our group's work on blood irradiation, NO photochemistry, and nanoparticle-mediated combination therapy.

proposals.title: A Novel Whole-Body Photobiomodulation System for Systemic Anti-Cancer and Anti-Metastatic Effects: Design, Dosimetry, and Preclinical Validation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others demonstrates that localized photobiomodulation (PBMt) can modulate tumor microenvironments, affect cancer stem cells (CSCs), and enhance nanoparticle-mediated therapies. However, treating disseminated metastatic disease or achieving systemic immunomodulatory effects requires light delivery beyond localized tumors. Current PBMt devices are designed for localized treatment, creating a critical technological gap for addressing systemic cancer progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a whole-body, low-fluence PBMt system, delivering specific red/NIR wavelengths, will induce systemic biological effects that inhibit metastatic spread, modulate circulating immune cells, and potentiate the activity of systemically administered therapeutic nanoparticles in pre-clinical cancer models.
proposals.method: We will engineer a whole-body light chamber for rodents using LED arrays emitting at 660nm and 810nm, calibrated for uniform whole-body fluence (e.g., 5-30 J/cm²). Dosimetry will be modeled using Monte Carlo simulation of light transport in multi-layered tissue. We will test this system in two syngeneic mouse models of metastatic breast cancer. Experimental groups will include: PBMt alone, PBMt combined with intravenous curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (inspired by prior work), and appropriate controls. Primary outcomes will be metastatic burden, circulating tumor cell counts, and profiling of systemic immune markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated platform and protocol for whole-body PBMt in oncology research, establishing critical dose-response data for systemic effects. It will directly test the paradigm of using light as a systemic adjuvant therapy, potentially unlocking new combinatorial strategies for metastatic disease and cancer immunotherapy based on pre-2026 mechanistic insights into PBMt.
proposals.novelty: While localized PBMt is well-studied, the deliberate engineering of a device for uniform whole-body delivery to treat systemic disease is novel. Combining this systemic light delivery with systemically administered therapeutic nanoparticles to create a synergistic, body-wide treatment modality represents a significant conceptual and technical advance beyond existing localized approaches.

proposals.title: Predictive Modeling of Chitosan Subclasses for Enhanced Photodynamic and Photobiomodulation Therapies in Cancer and Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: Chitosan is a versatile biopolymer widely used in photomedicine for drug delivery, nanoparticle synthesis, and wound dressings. However, its inconsistent biological performance, due to variable source materials and processing, hinders the reproducibility and efficacy of combinatorial therapies like photobiomodulation (PBMt) with chitosan-based drug carriers. A systematic, data-driven framework is urgently needed to correlate chitosan's physicochemical properties with specific therapeutic outcomes, moving beyond empirical formulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning analysis of a comprehensive chitosan library's physicochemical 'fingerprint' will reveal predictive structure-function relationships, enabling the rational definition of performance-based subclasses optimized for specific applications in cancer therapy (e.g., targeting CSCs) and wound healing.
proposals.method: We will generate a diverse chitosan library by varying deacetylation degree, molecular weight, and source. Each variant will be characterized via NMR, FTIR, and GPC. Biological assays will include antimicrobial activity, nanoparticle encapsulation efficiency for curcumin/NO donors, and effects on cancer cell spheroid (CSC model) viability post-PBMt. A Random Forest regression model will be trained to predict biological outcomes from the physicochemical data, with SHAP analysis identifying key predictive features for subclass definition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-access, predictive database linking chitosan structure to function in photomedical contexts. It will provide a rational basis for selecting chitosan subclasses to enhance combinatorial therapies, such as PBMt with nanoparticle-delivered agents, improving treatment reproducibility and efficacy against metastatic cancer and diabetic wounds.
proposals.novelty: While chitosan is commonly used, applying a machine learning-driven, high-throughput fingerprinting approach to define functional subclasses specifically for photomedicine is novel. This moves the field from trial-and-error formulation to predictive design, directly addressing reproducibility challenges in combinatorial therapy research.

proposals.title: Establishing a Standardized Chitosan Reference System and Bioassay Cascade for Reproducible Biomedical Research and Combination Therapy Development
proposals.motivation: Chitosan's variability in source, degree of deacetylation (DA), and molecular weight critically undermines reproducibility in biomedical research, particularly in photobiomodulation (PBMt) combination therapies and nanoparticle-based drug delivery for cancer and wound healing. The lack of standardized materials and validated potency assays prevents direct comparison between studies, hindering clinical translation and the optimization of synergistic treatments, such as chitosan-curcumin nanoparticles with PBMt. This mirrors the historical challenge in biopharmaceuticals before the establishment of international reference standards.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a panel of well-characterized chitosan reference materials, coupled with a validated, hierarchical assay cascade from physicochemical analysis to functional bioactivity, will provide a robust framework for standardizing chitosan characterization, enabling reliable correlation between material properties and biological outcomes in therapeutic applications.
proposals.method: We will produce and certify a panel of chitosan reference materials covering key variables (DA, molecular weight, source). A mandatory testing cascade will be developed: 1) Primary physicochemical identity via standardized SEC-MALS for molecular weight distribution and quantitative NMR for DA/pattern of acetylation (PA). 2) A functional biochemical assay using a standardized fluorescent anionic probe (e.g., pyranine) to quantify polycationic binding activity. 3) A standardized in vitro bioassay using a defined macrophage cell line (e.g., RAW 264.7) to measure a specific immune-modulatory response (e.g., TNF-α secretion) upon chitosan stimulation, establishing a potency unit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated reference system for chitosan, directly enabling reproducible research in PBMt combination therapies and nanomedicine. It will allow researchers to benchmark commercial chitosan batches, report bioactivity in standardized units, and establish clear structure-activity relationships, accelerating the rational design of chitosan-based therapeutics for cancer, antimicrobial applications, and wound healing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the proven biopharmaceutical standardization paradigm—used for proteins like cytokines—to the complex polysaccharide chitosan, creating a formalized reference material system and a mandatory, validated bioassay hierarchy specifically for biomedical polymer research, which has not been done before.

proposals.title: A Hierarchically Porous Chitosan Nanofiber Patch via Hybrid Electrospinning and Ice-Templating for Enhanced Diabetic Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: Chronic diabetic wounds are a major clinical challenge, characterized by excessive exudate, hypoxia, and impaired cell migration, leading to high risks of infection and amputation. Current wound dressings often fail to simultaneously manage fluid, promote oxygenation, and guide tissue regeneration. There is a critical need for advanced biomaterials that can actively modulate the wound microenvironment to accelerate healing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chitosan-based wound patch engineered with a dual-scale (nano/micro) hierarchical porous architecture will significantly improve wound healing outcomes in diabetic models by enhancing exudate drainage, oxygen diffusion, and guided cellular infiltration compared to conventional dressings or single-scale scaffolds.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a primary nanofibrous scaffold via electrospinning of a chitosan/poly(ethylene oxide) blend. This scaffold will then undergo a controlled ice-templating (freeze-drying) process, where a unidirectional thermal gradient will induce the growth of ice crystals, templating a secondary network of interconnected micro-channels within the nanofiber matrix. The resulting hybrid patch will be characterized using SEM, porosity measurements, and fluid uptake assays. Its efficacy will be tested in a streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat wound model, with animals randomly assigned to control, electrospun-only, and hybrid patch groups, assessing wound closure rates, histology, and markers of angiogenesis and inflammation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, scalable fabrication method for creating hierarchically structured wound dressings. It will provide direct pre-clinical evidence that integrating nano-scale fibrous networks with engineered micro-channels can synergistically address multiple pathological features of diabetic wounds, advancing the design principles for next-generation regenerative biomaterials.
proposals.novelty: While ice-templating is established for creating bulk macroporous scaffolds and electrospinning for nano-fibrous mats, their sequential hybrid application to create a single, integrated patch with defined hierarchical porosity for wound management is novel. This approach directly translates a materials design principle from energy storage to solve a core biomedical problem of scaling in tissue engineering.

proposals.title: Delineating the Chromophoric Roles of Opsins and Voltage-Gated Calcium Channels in the Anti-Inflammatory Effects of Photobiomodulation on Macrophages
proposals.motivation: Photobiomodulation (PBM) is a promising non-invasive therapy for reducing inflammation, but its primary molecular chromophores remain debated. Pre-2026 evidence suggests both opsins and calcium channels may be involved, yet their specific contributions and potential interactions are unclear. Elucidating this mechanism is critical for rational, target-specific optimization of PBM protocols for inflammatory conditions, including those complicating cancer and diabetic wound healing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PBM's anti-inflammatory effects in macrophages are initiated by the concurrent photoactivation of specific opsins (e.g., OPN3) and voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs), leading to a synergistic intracellular calcium ([Ca2+]i) transient that downregulates pro-inflammatory NF-κB signaling.
proposals.method: We will use RAW 264.7 macrophages stimulated with LPS. Cells will be treated with specific inhibitors (e.g., ω-conotoxin for N-type VGCCs) or siRNA knockdowns targeting candidate opsins prior to irradiation with 660nm and 810nm LEDs. Real-time [Ca2+]i flux will be quantified using Fluo-4 AM ratiometric imaging. Downstream effects will be assessed via Western blot for phospho-NF-κB p65 and ELISA for TNF-α. A factorial experimental design will analyze main effects and interactions of opsin and VGCC inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for the co-involvement and interaction of opsins and VGCCs as PBM chromophores in an immune cell model. It will establish a more precise mechanistic framework for PBM's anti-inflammatory action, moving beyond cytochrome c oxidase-centric models. The findings will inform wavelength and power selection for clinical PBM applications aimed at modulating inflammation.
proposals.novelty: While opsins and calcium fluxes have been separately implicated in light therapy, systematically dissecting their combined role as primary photoreceptors in PBM-induced immunomodulation is novel. The approach directly tests for interaction between these two candidate pathways, a key unanswered question in pre-2026 PBM photobiology.

proposals.title: Precision Photobiomodulation Protocols Targeting Mitochondrial Cytochrome c Oxidase to Modulate Microglial and Macrophage Polarization in Neuroinflammatory and Acute Lung Injury Models
proposals.motivation: Neuroinflammatory disorders (e.g., Alzheimer's disease) and acute lung injury (ALI)/ARDS are major health burdens with limited treatment options, both characterized by dysregulated inflammation and oxidative stress. Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others shows PBM can reduce inflammation via mitochondrial mechanisms, but optimal, tissue-specific protocols for modulating key immune cells (microglia, macrophages) remain undefined, hindering clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PBM parameters (wavelength, fluence, pulse structure) can be systematically optimized to differentially shift microglia and alveolar macrophages from a pro-inflammatory (M1) to an anti-inflammatory (M2) phenotype by selectively modulating mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase activity and downstream ROS/NO signaling.
proposals.method: We will use in vitro models of LPS-stimulated BV-2 microglia and RAW 264.7 macrophages. A full-factorial design of experiments (DoE) will test PBM parameters: wavelengths (660nm, 810nm, 980nm), fluences (1-30 J/cm²), and continuous vs. pulsed modes. High-content analysis will quantify M1/M2 markers (iNOS, Arg1) and mitochondrial membrane potential. Optimal in vitro parameters will be validated in murine models of LPS-induced neuroinflammation and ALI, using nanoparticle-based delivery of a mitochondrial-targeted photosensitizer (based on Hamblin's prior work with curcumin-iron oxide NPs) to enhance PBM specificity to immune cells. Outcomes include histology, cytokine profiling, and behavioral/respiratory function tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comparative, mechanism-based PBM parameter sets for brain versus lung inflammation, directly linking specific light parameters to immune cell polarization. It will provide a rational framework for designing non-pharmacological, adjuvant anti-inflammatory therapies and demonstrate a novel combinatorial strategy using targeted nanoparticles to increase PBM precision.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, systematic comparison of PBM effects on two distinct inflammatory milieus (brain vs. lung) using a DoE approach to optimize for immune phenotype switching, combined with a mitochondrial-targeting nanoparticle strategy to enhance cell specificity, moving beyond generic anti-inflammatory PBM effects.

proposals.title: Systemic Immunomodulation via Localized Photobiomodulation: A Preclinical Investigation in a Collagen-Induced Arthritis Model
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune diseases, driven by chronic systemic inflammation, represent a major therapeutic challenge with significant side effects from current immunosuppressive drugs. Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others demonstrates that localized PBM can reduce inflammation and promote healing in wounds and joints, and that PBM can influence circulating immune cells and induce anti-inflammatory cytokines. This suggests a potential for localized light to generate a beneficial systemic effect, a paradigm shift from direct tissue treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted, low-level PBM applied to a major lymphoid site (e.g., the spleen or a primary arthritic joint) will induce a sustained systemic anti-inflammatory response, mediated by circulating regulatory immune cells and soluble factors, leading to amelioration of distal, untreated inflammatory disease sites.
proposals.method: We will use a DBA/1 mouse model of collagen-induced arthritis (CIA). Animals will be randomized into four groups: 1) Sham irradiation control, 2) Local PBM to a primary inflamed paw only (810nm laser, 50mW/cm², 3J/cm²), 3) Splenic PBM (same parameters transcutaneously), and 4) Combined PBM. Treatment will be administered daily post-disease onset. Systemic effects will be quantified by longitudinal clinical scoring of all paws, serum multiplex cytokine analysis (focusing on IL-10, TGF-β, TNF-α, IL-6), and flow cytometric profiling of circulating and splenic Treg (CD4+CD25+FoxP3+) and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs). Histopathology of treated and untreated joints will be the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence that localized PBM can be leveraged for whole-body immunomodulation in a classic autoimmune model. It will identify key systemic mediators (cellular and humoral) of the abscopal anti-inflammatory effect. The findings could establish a foundational rationale for using non-invasive PBM as an adjunctive, systemic therapy for autoimmune and chronic inflammatory conditions.
proposals.novelty: While PBM's local effects are well-documented, the intentional exploitation of these effects to program a sustained systemic immune response for treating remote disease is novel. The combined approach of targeting both a disease site and a central immune organ (spleen) to amplify systemic effects is an innovative strategy not previously explored in the PBM field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Dual-Responsive Magnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles for Photobiomodulation-Enhanced Tissue Repair and Cancer Stem Cell Targeting via Shared Microenvironmental Triggers
proposals.motivation: Current smart nanotherapeutics for cancer, especially those targeting cancer stem cells (CSCs), are often designed for unique tumor microenvironment (TME) triggers like extreme hypoxia or abnormal pH, limiting their broader applicability. However, many TME hallmarks, such as moderate hypoxia, elevated ROS, and specific proteases, are also transiently present in normal wound healing and inflammatory repair processes. Exploiting these shared triggers could enable a single, multi-functional nanoparticle platform for both regenerative medicine and oncology, a concept underexplored pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs), co-loaded with a therapeutic agent (e.g., curcumin) and surface-functionalized with dual-responsive ligands, can be activated by microenvironmental stimuli (e.g., ROS and matrix metalloproteinases-MMPs) common to both diabetic wounds and tumors, enabling photobiomodulation (PBM)-enhanced tissue repair and, with adjusted parameters, CSC suppression.
proposals.method: We will synthesize curcumin-loaded IONPs and coat them with a dual-responsive shell: a MMP-9 cleavable peptide (abundant in both chronic wounds and tumors) linked to a ROS-sensitive thioketal group. In vitro, using controlled cell culture groups (primary fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and patient-derived CSCs), we will test PBM (660nm/810nm) effects on nanoparticle uptake, cargo release, and cell fate (proliferation vs. apoptosis) under normoxic, hypoxic, and high-ROS conditions. In vivo, we will use two diabetic rat models: a standard wound healing model and a tumor xenograft model with CSCs, comparing groups treated with nanoparticles alone, PBM alone, and their combination, assessing outcomes via histology and molecular markers for repair (e.g., VEGF, collagen) and CSC control (e.g., CD44, Oct-4).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a 'theranostic' nanoparticle platform whose function is dictated by the biological context of its stimulus, broadening the application of IONPs beyond cancer. It will deliver novel data on how PBM parameters can be tuned to either promote repair (in wounds) or inhibit metastasis (in tumors) using the same agent, directly extending Dr. Hamblin's prior work on PBM-nanoparticle combinations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately designing a nanoplatform for shared, not unique, pathological triggers, creating a single intervention testable in two distinct disease models (repair and oncology). This contrasts with the prevailing pre-2026 focus on tumor-exclusive targeting and integrates PBM as a dynamic control switch for the platform's function.

proposals.title: Photobiomodulation-Triggered, Synthetic Biology-Engineered Bacterial Vectors for Targeted Therapy of Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, and their resistance to conventional therapies remains a major clinical challenge. Pre-2026 work by Hamblin's group and others has shown that photobiomodulation (PBM) can modulate the tumor microenvironment and that nanoparticles can be used for targeted drug delivery, but these systems often lack the sophisticated, multi-stimulus responsiveness needed for precise CSC targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering non-pathogenic bacteria with synthetic AND-logic gates, responsive to both tumor microenvironment cues (e.g., hypoxia) and an external PBM light signal, will enable spatiotemporally controlled release of anti-CSC therapeutics, thereby improving treatment specificity and efficacy.
proposals.method: We will engineer Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 with a synthetic genetic circuit. The circuit will incorporate a hypoxia-responsive promoter (e.g., pFNR) AND a light-responsive promoter (e.g., pDawn) upstream of a therapeutic payload gene (e.g., a CSC-targeting drug or prodrug-converting enzyme). The bacteria will be loaded with a photosensitizer (e.g., curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles, as per Hamblin's prior work) to amplify local reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation upon PBM (670 nm). In vitro, we will test the system's specificity and cytotoxicity against CSC-enriched spheroids, comparing treatment groups (bacteria only, PBM only, combined) using established metastasis assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a living, logic-gated drug delivery platform that integrates Hamblin's expertise in PBM and nanoparticle-mediated therapy. It will provide a novel strategy to combat CSCs by combining the tumor-homing ability of bacteria with the precise external control of PBM, potentially reducing off-target effects. The findings will contribute to the fields of synthetic biology and translational photomedicine.
proposals.novelty: While bacterial therapies and PBM are both known, their integration via a genetically encoded AND-logic gate for conditional, dual-stimulus drug release is novel. This approach moves beyond passive nanoparticles to create an active, biologically integrated system responsive to the dynamic tumor microenvironment and an externally applied therapeutic light dose.

proposals.title: Optogenetically-Engineered Magnetic Nanoparticles for Spatiotemporally Controlled Drug Release and Cancer Stem Cell Targeting
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, but their eradication is limited by the inability to precisely control therapeutic release within tumors. Pre-2026 work by Hamblin's group shows that combining photobiomodulation (PBM) with nanoparticles improves outcomes, yet release mechanisms (e.g., heat, ROS) are indirect and lack molecular specificity. Integrating the precise, optical gating of optogenetics into nanomedicine could revolutionize targeted therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with light-sensitive channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) variants or light-oxygen-voltage (LOV) domains will enable precise, low-power optical control of ion flux or conformational changes to trigger the targeted release of CSC-specific therapeutics (e.g., curcumin, salinomycin) in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.method: We will synthesize carboxylated iron oxide MNPs and conjugate them, via EDC/NHS chemistry, to engineered ChR2 mutants (e.g., CatCh for Ca2+ permeability) or LOV2-Jα photoswitchable proteins. Drug loading will be via encapsulation or covalent tethering cleavable by light-induced conformational change. Release kinetics will be quantified under 470nm (blue) and 660nm (red) light exposure, relevant to PBM wavelengths. Efficacy will be tested in CSC-enriched breast cancer spheroids and a murine metastasis model, comparing treatment groups (light + NPs, NPs alone, light alone) for tumor burden and CSC markers (CD44+/CD24-).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for 'optogenetic nanomedicine,' merging the spatial precision of optogenetics with the targeting ability of MNPs. It will provide a direct method to optically control drug release with molecular specificity, potentially improving CSC eradication and offering a new paradigm for combinatorial PBM therapies.
proposals.novelty: While light-triggered nanocarriers exist (e.g., based on photocleavable linkers or photothermal effects), this approach is novel by directly integrating functional, light-gated ion channels/proteins onto MNPs to create a biohybrid system that uses physiological signaling (e.g., Ca2+ influx) for controlled release, a concept not yet realized in pre-2026 nanomedicine.

proposals.title: Photobiomodulation-Triggered Release of Curcumin from Self-Healing Polymer Nanoparticles for Targeting Breast Cancer Stem Cells and Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, and are notoriously resistant to conventional therapies. Pre-2026 work by Hamblin's group shows that combining photobiomodulation (PBMt) with nanoparticle-delivered agents like curcumin can enhance therapeutic outcomes, but achieving precise, on-demand drug release at tumor sites remains a challenge. Stimuli-responsive self-healing polymers, which can reversibly break and reform bonds, offer a promising platform for controlled release but have not been integrated with PBMt for CSC targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating curcumin within self-healing polymer nanoparticles whose dynamic bonds are cleaved by PBMt-generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) will enable spatiotemporally controlled drug release, leading to enhanced eradication of breast CSCs and inhibition of metastasis in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.method: We will synthesize nanoparticles from a self-healing polymer network based on reversible disulfide bonds and boronic ester linkages, known to be sensitive to oxidative stress. Curcumin will be encapsulated during nanoparticle formation. We will test the system in vitro using breast cancer cell lines and CSC-enriched populations, applying PBMt (660-810 nm) to trigger ROS-mediated bond cleavage and curcumin release. Treatment groups will include: 1) nanoparticles + PBMt, 2) nanoparticles alone, 3) free curcumin + PBMt, and 4) controls. Outcomes will be assessed via CSC marker expression (CD44+/CD24-), tumorsphere formation, migration/invasion assays, and apoptosis. An orthotopic mouse model of breast cancer metastasis will be used for in vivo validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel combinatorial platform that merges PBMt with smart material science for targeted cancer therapy. It will provide proof-of-concept that PBMt can be used as a precise external trigger to control drug release from next-generation nanoparticles. The findings could lead to a new strategy for overcoming CSC-mediated therapy resistance and preventing metastatic spread.
proposals.novelty: While PBMt has been combined with static nanoparticles, its use as a direct trigger to break dynamic covalent bonds in self-healing polymers for drug release is unexplored. The novelty lies in designing a material whose healing/release properties are explicitly controlled by the photochemical ROS generation inherent to PBMt, creating a synergistic, on-demand therapy.

proposals.title: Single-Cell-Triggered Drug Release from Multifunctional Magnetic Nanoparticles for Targeting Cancer Stem Cells in Metastatic Models
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis but are notoriously resistant to conventional therapies. Pre-2026 work shows that combination therapies, like photobiomodulation (PBM) with nanoparticle-delivered agents, can improve outcomes, but current systems lack the precision to target individual CSCs within a heterogeneous tumor mass. This limits efficacy and increases off-target toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multifunctional nanoparticles (MNPs) engineered to release a dual payload (e.g., a CSC-targeting drug and a nitric oxide donor) in direct response to single-cell-level stimuli (specifically, CSC-associated enzymatic activity) will selectively eradicate CSCs and inhibit metastasis more effectively than bulk-triggered systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide core-shell MNPs, co-loaded with curcumin and a nitro ruthenium-based NO precursor. The shell will incorporate a labile linker cleavable by the CSC-associated enzyme ALDH1A1. Using in vitro 3D spheroid models of metastatic breast cancer, we will apply a controlled PBM protocol (660 nm) to the MNPs. Single-cell analysis (live-cell imaging, ROS/NO fluorescence probes) will confirm payload release triggered by individual ALDH1A1+ cells within the spheroid. Efficacy will be assessed by measuring CSC depletion (via flow cytometry for CD44+/CD24-), spheroid disintegration, and inhibition of migration in a transwell assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for single-cell-targeted combination therapy, directly addressing CSC heterogeneity. It will contribute a novel MNP design that integrates enzymatic sensing with PBM-enhanced action, providing a platform to study and combat metastasis-initiating cells with unprecedented spatial precision.
proposals.novelty: While stimulus-responsive nanoparticles exist, the novelty lies in engineering the release mechanism to be activated by a biochemical signature of a single target cell, not a bulk tissue environment, and combining this with PBM to synergistically enhance the cytotoxicity of a dual chemotherapeutic/nitric oxide payload.

proposals.title: Low-Level Laser Therapy Activates Dermal Stem Cells via Mitochondrial ROS-Mediated PI3K/Akt and Wnt/β-Catenin Signaling Pathways
proposals.motivation: Photobiomodulation (PBM) therapy, or low-level laser therapy (LLLT), is a promising non-invasive modality for enhancing wound healing and tissue regeneration, effects attributed in part to stem cell activation. However, the precise molecular signaling pathways by which LLLT photonic energy is transduced in dermal stem cells remain poorly mapped. Elucidating these pathways is critical for optimizing therapeutic parameters and developing rational combinatorial treatments, especially for chronic wounds and aging skin where stem cell function is impaired.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLLT at specific red/NIR wavelengths activates resident dermal stem/progenitor cells primarily through the generation of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mtROS), which subsequently trigger key pro-proliferative and pro-survival pathways, specifically PI3K/Akt and Wnt/β-catenin signaling.
proposals.method: We will isolate and culture primary murine dermal stem cells (mDSCs) and human dermal papilla cells. Cells will be irradiated using a 660nm and 810nm diode laser at fluences (0-10 J/cm²) previously shown to be stimulatory. Using specific fluorescent probes (MitoSOX Red), we will quantify mtROS production post-LLLT. Pathway activation will be mapped via western blotting for phosphorylated Akt (p-Akt) and stabilized/non-phosphorylated β-catenin, and validated using specific pharmacological inhibitors (LY294002 for PI3K/Akt; XAV939 for Wnt/β-catenin). Functional stem cell activation will be assessed via colony-forming unit assays, proliferation (BrdU), and migration (scratch assay), comparing control, LLLT-only, and LLLT + inhibitor groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed mechanistic map linking LLLT's biophysical stimulus to specific stem cell activation pathways in the skin. It will identify optimal parameters for stem cell stimulation and validate key nodal points (mtROS, Akt, β-catenin) as targets for enhancing or modulating the PBM effect. This foundational knowledge will directly inform the design of more effective PBM-based therapies for regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: While LLLT's effects on mitochondrial function are known, a systematic dissection of the downstream signaling cascade specifically in dermal stem cells, connecting mtROS to the concurrent activation of both PI3K/Akt and Wnt pathways, has not been performed. This integrated pathway analysis is novel and moves beyond phenomenological observations of increased cell growth.

proposals.title: Inverse Photobiomodulation: Systematic Determination of Light Parameters to Inhibit Cancer Stem Cell-Driven Wound Healing and Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Photobiomodulation (PBM) is widely studied for its pro-healing effects, but its potential to deliberately inhibit healing in pathological contexts is underexplored. This is critical for cancer therapy, where surgical wound healing can inadvertently promote local recurrence and metastasis, potentially driven by activated cancer stem cells (CSCs). Pre-2026 work by Hamblin's group on PBM, nanoparticles, and metastasis control provides a foundation to investigate this inverse application.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, non-thermal light parameters (wavelength, fluence, pulse structure) can be identified that suppress CSC proliferation and impair healing processes in tumor microenvironment models, and these effects can be enhanced by combination with photodynamic or photothermal agents.
proposals.method: We will employ an in vitro high-throughput screening platform using 2D and 3D co-cultures of CSCs and fibroblasts. A tunable LED/pulsed laser system will systematically vary parameters (630-850 nm, 1-50 J/cm², CW vs. pulsed). Primary output measures will be CSC viability (flow cytometry for CD44+/CD24-), colony formation, and secretion of pro-healing cytokines (ELISA). An iterative, feedback-controlled algorithm will map the parameter space to identify inhibitory combinations. Promising regimens will be tested in a diabetic rat wound model inoculated with cancer cells, combined with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles to assess synergistic effects on delaying healing and reducing metastatic burden.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'inverse PBM' parameter library for selectively impairing pathological healing. It will provide a direct translational strategy to use light as an adjuvant to cancer surgery to mitigate recurrence risks. The findings will expand the therapeutic duality of light-based interventions beyond stimulation.
proposals.novelty: While vast literature seeks optimal light for healing, this proposal systematically inverts the problem to find inhibitory parameters, a non-obvious application of PBM principles. Combining this inverse approach with nanoparticle-mediated photodynamic therapy, as hinted at in Hamblin's prior work, creates a novel combinatorial anti-cancer modality.

proposals.title: Combined Photobiomodulation and Topical Calcipotriene Delivery for Psoriasis: A Mechanistic Study on Synergistic Modulation of Keratinocyte Proliferation and Inflammatory Pathways
proposals.motivation: Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by hyperproliferation of keratinocytes and dysregulated immune responses. Current topical treatments, such as vitamin D analogs (e.g., calcipotriene), can be limited by variable efficacy and skin irritation. Pre-2026 evidence shows that low-level laser therapy (LLLT, or photobiomodulation - PBM) can reduce inflammation and modulate cell proliferation, suggesting a potential synergistic role when combined with topical drug delivery to enhance therapeutic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-treatment with specific PBM parameters will enhance the transdermal delivery and cellular uptake of topical calcipotriene, leading to a synergistic suppression of keratinocyte hyperproliferation and pro-inflammatory cytokine production (e.g., IL-17, TNF-α) compared to either treatment alone.
proposals.method: We will use an imiquimod-induced psoriatic plaque model in mice. Animals will be divided into four groups: control (sham), PBM alone (660 nm, 50 mW/cm², 3 J/cm²), topical calcipotriene alone, and combined PBM (applied 30 minutes prior to drug). Drug delivery will be quantified using fluorescently tagged calcipotriene analogs and confocal microscopy. Key outcomes include histological scoring (PASI), immunohistochemistry for Ki-67 and CD3+ T cells, and qPCR analysis of inflammatory cytokines from skin biopsies. Nanoparticle-enhanced delivery, building on the PI's prior work with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles, will be explored in a secondary arm using calcipotriene-loaded liposomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide mechanistic evidence for a novel, non-invasive combinatorial therapy for psoriasis. It will delineate the optical parameters that optimize drug delivery and define the molecular pathways (proliferative and immunomodulatory) through which PBM synergizes with a standard topical agent. The findings could directly inform the design of future clinical protocols.
proposals.novelty: While PBM and topical drugs have been studied separately, the systematic investigation of PBM as a physical primer to enhance the efficacy of a specific, first-line anti-psoriatic drug (calcipotriene) is novel. The approach integrates the PI's expertise in PBM mechanisms and nanoparticle-mediated delivery into a focused dermatological application.

proposals.title: Photobiomodulation-Enhanced Nanotherapy for Rapid Viral Decontamination: A Novel Strategy to Shift Pandemic Control from Containment to Treatment
proposals.motivation: Traditional pandemic control relies heavily on non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) like lockdowns, which carry severe socioeconomic costs. Pre-2026, antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) and photobiomodulation (PBM) have shown promise for inactivating pathogens and modulating immune responses. This proposal investigates if enhancing therapeutic decontamination can reduce reliance on disruptive NPIs by making rapid, localized treatment a viable primary control pillar for respiratory viruses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combined treatment of PBM and nanoparticle-mediated aPDT will achieve rapid, broad-spectrum viral inactivation in the upper respiratory tract and in aerosols, significantly reducing viral load and transmission potential more effectively than either modality alone.
proposals.method: We will synthesize curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (C-IONPs) functionalized with viral entry inhibitors. Using in vitro models of human respiratory epithelial cells and 3D airway cultures, we will test the combined protocol: first, low-level laser PBM (e.g., 660nm, 50mW/cm²) to prime cellular defenses and increase nanoparticle uptake, followed by aPDT activation of C-IONPs (using 425nm blue light) to generate localized ROS for viral inactivation. Viral titers (e.g., of a model beta-coronavirus) will be quantified by plaque assay. Aerosol decontamination will be tested in a controlled chamber, measuring viral load reduction post-treatment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel, non-invasive therapeutic strategy aimed at directly reducing the infectious reservoir in early-stage patients and contaminated environments. It will generate critical data on the synergistic efficacy of PBM and nanomaterial-based aPDT against enveloped viruses, contributing to the field of light-based antimicrobials and pandemic preparedness.
proposals.novelty: While PBM and aPDT have been studied separately, their sequential combination for enhanced antiviral effect, specifically targeting the upper respiratory tract to curb transmission at its source, is a novel application. This approach repurposes Hamblin's established nanoparticle-PBM synergy from wound healing to a new domain of infectious disease control.

proposals.title: Photobiomodulation and Curcumin-Loaded Nanoparticles Modulate Host Macrophage Polarization to Suppress Cancer Stem Cell-Driven Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, often evading conventional therapies that directly target tumor bulk. The host's tumor microenvironment, particularly immunosuppressive M2-like tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), supports CSC survival and spread. Pre-2026 work by Hamblin's group shows the potential of combining photobiomodulation (PBMt) and curcumin-loaded nanoparticles for wound healing, suggesting a powerful, non-toxic modality for modulating host biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined application of PBMt and curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles will reprogram the host immune response by polarizing macrophages from a pro-tumor M2 phenotype to an anti-tumor M1 phenotype, thereby disrupting the supportive niche for CSCs and inhibiting metastatic progression.
proposals.method: We will use a syngeneic mouse model of metastatic breast cancer (e.g., 4T1). Animals will be randomized into four groups: control, PBMt alone (using a 660/810nm polychromatic LED device), curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (Cur-IONPs) alone, and the combined treatment. Primary tumor growth and lung metastasis will be quantified. Flow cytometry and immunohistochemistry will analyze tumor-infiltrating macrophages (F4/80, CD86 for M1, CD206 for M2) and CSC markers (CD44+/CD24-). In vitro, co-cultures of CSCs with bone marrow-derived macrophages will assess the direct effect of treatments on macrophage polarization and its impact on CSC sphere formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, host-directed therapeutic strategy against metastasis by repurposing a non-invasive PBMt-nanoparticle combination. It will provide mechanistic insight into how this combination therapy remodels the tumor immune microenvironment to target CSCs. The findings could translate into an adjuvant therapy to prevent recurrence after primary tumor resection.
proposals.novelty: While PBMt and curcumin have been studied separately in oncology, their combination for specific immunomodulation of TAMs to target CSCs is unexplored. This proposal innovatively inverts the focus from directly killing cancer cells to reprogramming the host's immune response using a pre-clinically validated, low-toxicity combinatorial platform.

proposals.title: Shifting the Paradigm in Photodynamic Therapy: A Protocol for Vascular-Targeted PDT with Immune Priming to Suppress Metastasis in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) for cancer focuses on direct tumor cell cytotoxicity, which can be limited by heterogeneous photosensitizer uptake and incomplete ablation, potentially leaving behind treatment-resistant cancer stem cells (CSCs) that drive recurrence and metastasis. Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others highlights the critical secondary effects of PDT, including vascular damage and immunogenic cell death, suggesting these mechanisms could be strategically harnessed as primary treatment goals for more robust and systemic anti-tumor effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a PDT protocol specifically designed to induce rapid vascular shutdown in the primary tumor, followed by a low-dose 'immuno-stimulatory' light fraction, will more effectively control local disease and prime a systemic anti-tumor immune response to suppress metastatic spread than a standard cytotoxic PDT protocol.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage light delivery protocol in a murine 4T1 triple-negative breast cancer model. First, a high-fluence-rate 670nm laser will be administered shortly after intravenous injection of the vascular-targeting photosensitizer Visudyne (verteporfin) to induce rapid vascular occlusion. Second, after a 24-hour delay to allow immune cell infiltration, a low-fluence-rate 810nm photobiomodulation (PBM) treatment will be applied to the same site to modulate the tumor microenvironment. We will use in vivo imaging to monitor vascular shutdown, flow cytometry to analyze immune cell populations (particularly CD8+ T cells and dendritic cells), and a lung metastasis assay to quantify systemic efficacy. Nanoparticle-based curcumin, known from Hamblin's prior work to enhance wound healing, will be tested as an adjuvant to amplify the immune response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, optimized PDT protocol that explicitly prioritizes vascular and immune effects over direct cytotoxicity. It will deliver quantitative evidence on how such a strategy impacts metastatic burden and long-term survival. The findings could establish a new standard for combinatorial PDT design, moving the field toward treatments that address both the primary tumor and systemic disease.
proposals.novelty: While vascular and immune effects of PDT are known, deliberately designing a clinical protocol where they are the primary, sequenced objectives—using a vascular-photosensitizer followed by PBM for immune priming—is a novel and non-obvious therapeutic strategy. This approach directly challenges the canonical goal of maximizing direct tumor cell kill.

proposals.title: Modulation of Photodynamic Therapy-Induced Inflammatory Signaling to Elicit Systemic Anti-Tumor Immunity and Control Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is known to induce localized inflammation and immunogenic cell death, but its potential to generate a robust, systemic anti-tumor immune response capable of targeting distant metastases remains incompletely harnessed. Pre-2026 work highlights the critical role of the tumor microenvironment and cancer stem cells (CSCs) in treatment resistance and recurrence. This proposal addresses the need to convert PDT's acute pro-inflammatory effects into a durable, adaptive immune memory against residual and disseminated disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted modulation of the inflammatory signaling pathways activated by PDT, using specific pharmacological or nanoparticle-based agents, will shift the immune response from a transient, suppressive state to a potent, systemic CD8+ T-cell-driven immunity, thereby eradicating primary tumors and inhibiting metastatic outgrowth.
proposals.method: We will employ a syngeneic mouse model of metastatic breast cancer (4T1-Luc2). PDT will be performed using a polychromatic light source and a clinically relevant photosensitizer. The inflammatory milieu post-PDT will be modulated using two strategies: 1) systemic administration of a selective COX-2 inhibitor to dampen immunosuppressive prostaglandins, and 2) intratumoral injection of curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (inspired by recent wound healing work) to further promote immunogenic cell death and antigen presentation. Tumor growth, metastasis (via bioluminescence), and immune cell infiltrates (flow cytometry of tumor, spleen, blood) will be compared across four groups: Control, PDT alone, PDT + COX-2 inhibitor, and PDT + nanoparticles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic understanding of how to steer PDT-induced inflammation towards a therapeutically beneficial systemic immune response. It will deliver a practical, combinatory treatment protocol designed to enhance the abscopal effect and target CSCs within the metastatic niche. The findings could directly inform the design of next-generation PDT clinical trials aimed at preventing recurrence and metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While PDT's immunogenic effects are recognized, the deliberate pharmacological and nanomaterial-based reprogramming of its specific inflammatory signaling cascade to overcome immunosuppression and target metastasis is novel. Combining PDT with immunomodulatory nanoparticles, building on prior success in diabetic wound models, represents a unique translational strategy.

proposals.title: Targeted Ablation of Single Cancer Stem Cells Using a Microfluidic Photodynamic Therapy Platform with Curcumin-Loaded Nanoparticles
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor initiation, therapy resistance, and metastasis, but their study is hampered by the inability to selectively eliminate them within heterogeneous populations. Conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) lacks the spatial precision for single-cell manipulation, limiting research into CSC resilience and population dynamics. An ultra-precise 'micro-PDT' system would enable controlled ablation of individual CSCs, providing a powerful tool for fundamental oncology research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a microfluidic cell-trapping platform with precisely targeted laser excitation and nanoparticle-mediated photosensitizer delivery will enable the selective, light-triggered ablation of single cancer stem cells with minimal collateral damage to neighboring non-CSC tumor cells.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic chip with hydrodynamic traps to immobilize single cells from a co-culture of CSCs and bulk tumor cells. Cells will be pre-loaded with curcumin-conjugated iron oxide nanoparticles, leveraging our group's prior expertise. A confocal laser scanning microscope, modified for 450 nm irradiation, will provide sub-cellular targeting. A custom control algorithm will map cell positions, direct the laser to specified traps, and administer calibrated light doses (0.5-5 J/cm²). Real-time viability will be assessed via propidium iodide influx.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first integrated platform for the targeted photodynamic ablation of single cells within a micro-environment. It will provide quantitative data on the differential phototoxicity thresholds of CSCs versus non-CSCs. The methodology will serve as a new research tool for probing cell-cell communication, CSC niche interactions, and the dynamics of tumor repopulation post-selective ablation.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell manipulation exists via optogenetics or lasers, applying the principles of PDT for selective pharmacological ablation at the single-cell level is novel. The combination of a microfluidic trap array, nanoparticle-based photosensitizer targeting, and algorithmic laser control for PDT is a unique and previously unreported synthesis of technologies.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of Targeted Nanoparticle-Mediated Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy in Complex, Multi-Species Infection Models of Diabetic Wounds and Burn Sepsis
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance is a critical pre-2026 global health threat, demanding novel non-antibiotic therapies. While antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) shows promise in vitro and in simple rodent models, its translation is hindered by a lack of data in clinically relevant, complex animal models that mimic human disease pathophysiology, such as polymicrobial infections in immunocompromised hosts. Expanding the validation of aPDT to these challenging models is essential to demonstrate its real-world potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aPDT, especially when enhanced with targeted photosensitizer-loaded nanoparticles, will be effective in eradicating or significantly reducing bacterial burden in complex, multi-species infection models that are refractory to conventional antibiotics, leading to improved healing outcomes.
proposals.method: We will establish two distinct, pre-clinically relevant animal models: a polymicrobial (e.g., Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus) infected diabetic rat wound model and a burn sepsis model with systemic bacterial dissemination. Building on Dr. Hamblin's prior work with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles, we will develop and characterize targeted nanoparticle conjugates (e.g., with antibodies or peptides) to enhance bacterial binding. Animals will be randomly assigned to control (untreated, antibiotic-only), free photosensitizer aPDT, and nanoparticle-aPDT groups. Treatment efficacy will be assessed through longitudinal bioluminescence imaging for bacterial load, quantitative bacteriology, histopathology, and measurements of key inflammatory and healing markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical pre-clinical data on aPDT efficacy in complex, translationally relevant infection scenarios, moving beyond simplistic single-species models. It will establish a direct comparison between conventional and nanoparticle-enhanced aPDT in vivo, identifying the most promising formulation for challenging infections. The findings will significantly advance the field by defining the boundaries and optimal parameters for aPDT in realistic disease settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic evaluation of targeted nanoparticle-aPDT in two distinct, high-complexity animal models of infection that incorporate critical clinical features like polymicrobial communities, compromised host immunity (diabetes), and systemic involvement (sepsis), which have been minimally explored in the aPDT literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Dual-Wavelength Photodynamic Therapy Protocol Combining Methylene Blue and Curcumin-Loaded Nanoparticles for Concurrent Bacterial Eradication and Pro-Healing Stimulation in Diabetic Wounds
proposals.motivation: Chronic diabetic wounds are plagued by persistent biofilm infections and impaired healing, leading to high morbidity and amputation risk. Current antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) often uses high-dose photosensitizers and light that can damage host tissue, while separate photobiomodulation (PBM) for healing lacks direct anti-biofilm efficacy. A unified strategy that simultaneously addresses infection and dysfunctional repair is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential, dual-wavelength PDT protocol, using a bactericidal photosensitizer (methylene blue) followed by a pro-healing, nanoparticle-delivered agent (curcumin), will synergistically eradicate multidrug-resistant biofilms while directly stimulating fibroblast migration and angiogenesis in diabetic wound models.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage light delivery system. First, 660 nm red light will activate methylene blue targeting Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus biofilms in vitro and in a diabetic rat wound model. Second, 450 nm blue light will activate curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (building on our prior synthesis methods) to modulate reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels towards pro-healing signaling. Experimental groups will include controls, single treatments, and the combined protocol, with outcomes assessed via colony counts, ROS assays, immunohistochemistry for VEGF and CD31, and wound closure rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, translatable light dosing and drug delivery protocol that explicitly decouples the cytotoxic and cytoprotective roles of PDT. It will provide mechanistic insight into how sequential photo-activation can be tuned to first kill pathogens and then shift the wound microenvironment towards regeneration, directly addressing a major limitation of conventional aPDT.
proposals.novelty: While combined PDT/PBM has been explored, the novelty lies in the specific use of two distinct photosensitizers activated by different wavelengths in a timed sequence, and the application of our previously developed curcumin nanoparticles not just as a drug carrier but as a key photosensitizer component of the pro-healing phase.

proposals.title: Targeting Cancer Stem Cells with a Dual-Activatable Photosensitizer-Nanoparticle Platform for Precision Photodynamic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis but are notoriously resistant to conventional therapies. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is promising, but its efficacy against CSCs is limited by poor specificity, leading to off-target damage and suboptimal therapeutic outcomes. A strategy to precisely activate photosensitizers only within the unique CSC niche is critically needed to improve PDT's therapeutic index.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a photosensitizer engineered with two independent quenching mechanisms—one cleaved by a CSC-surface target (e.g., CD44) and the second by a CSC-microenvironment enzyme (e.g., matrix metalloproteinase-9)—will exhibit AND-gate logic, resulting in highly specific phototoxic activation exclusively within CSCs, thereby suppressing tumor growth and metastasis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a chlorin e6-based photosensitizer conjugated to iron oxide nanoparticles (leveraging prior expertise). The PS will be dual-quenched via a CD44-targeting peptide linker (cleavable upon binding) and a MMP-9-sensitive peptide sequence. Specificity and activation will be tested in vitro using co-cultures of CSCs and non-CSCs, assessing ROS generation and cytotoxicity post-irrigation with a 670nm diode laser (common in Hamblin's PBMT work). In vivo efficacy will be evaluated in orthotopic breast cancer models, monitoring primary tumor control and metastatic burden in treated versus control groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, highly specific therapeutic platform for eradicating CSCs, directly addressing a major bottleneck in cancer treatment. It will advance the field of targeted PDT by introducing a robust dual-lock activation mechanism, potentially reducing off-target effects seen with single-target approaches. The integration with therapeutic nanoparticles also aligns with combinatory strategies to enhance treatment outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While enzyme-activated or target-bound PS exist, the integration of both cues in an AND-gate configuration for CSC targeting is novel. This approach moves beyond simple delivery to require two disease-specific signals for activation, a concept not fully explored in pre-2026 PDT literature for CSC eradication.

proposals.title: Target-Activated Photosensitizer Probes for Selective Photodynamic Therapy of Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) is the reliance on differential photosensitizer (PS) accumulation in target tissue, which is often insufficient for eradicating cancer stem cells (CSCs) that drive metastasis and recurrence. Non-specific PS uptake in healthy tissue can lead to prolonged photosensitivity and off-target damage. This problem is particularly acute for CSCs, which are often less accessible and more resistant to standard therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that photosensitizer molecules engineered to be optically silent until they bind to a specific CSC surface marker (e.g., CD44 or CD133) will enable highly selective PDT, eliminating the need for high differential accumulation and minimizing damage to normal cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) conjugated with a CSC-targeting ligand (e.g., anti-CD44 antibody) and a novel, activatable PS. The PS will be a ruthenium-nitrosyl (Ru-NO) complex, similar to pre-2026 vasorelaxant agents, where photo-induced nitric oxide (NO) release is quenched by a fluorescence-quenching moiety linked via a cleavable peptide substrate specific to CSC-associated proteases (e.g., cathepsin B). Upon binding to CSCs and cleavage by the target protease, the quenching moiety is released, activating the PS for localized NO and ROS production upon 630-670 nm photobiomodulation (PBM) light irradiation. In vitro studies will use control groups of non-targeted nanoparticles and free PS in CSC-enriched vs. bulk cancer cell cultures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel therapeutic strategy to selectively target and eliminate CSCs with minimal off-target effects, directly addressing a key challenge in cancer treatment. It will demonstrate a proof-of-concept for 'binding-dependent' PS activation, moving the field beyond pharmacokinetic accumulation. The combination of target-activated PS with IONPs also offers potential for multimodal imaging and therapy.
proposals.novelty: While activatable probes exist for fluorescence imaging, their application to create truly target-activated photosensitizers for PDT, particularly against CSCs, is novel. The approach uniquely combines a ruthenium-nitrosyl photochemistry platform (for NO/ROS) with a protease-activated design, diverging from traditional porphyrin-based PSs and passive targeting methods.

proposals.title: A Modular, Clickable Platform for Rapid Generation of Targeted Photodynamic Therapy Conjugates Against Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy is limited by poor selectivity for malignant cells, leading to off-target damage and suboptimal treatment of cancer stem cells (CSCs) that drive metastasis and recurrence. While targeted PDT conjugates (e.g., antibody-photosensitizer) exist, their development is slow and bespoke, hindering systematic exploration of targeting ligands. A platform for rapidly generating diverse, well-defined targeted PDT agents is critically needed to improve specificity and advance combinatorial treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the modular, heterobifunctional linker concept from PROTAC technology will enable the rapid, bioorthogonal assembly of targeted PDT conjugates, and these modular agents will demonstrate superior selectivity and efficacy against CSCs in vitro and in vivo compared to non-targeted photosensitizers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a core photosensitizer (e.g., Chlorin e6 or a phthalocyanine derivative) functionalized with a terminal azide or tetrazine group. In parallel, we will create a library of targeting ligands (e.g., anti-CD44, anti-CD133 antibodies, or EGFR-binding peptides) bearing complementary bioorthogonal handles (cyclooctyne or trans-cyclooctene). Conjugation will be performed via strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition or inverse electron-demand Diels-Alder reactions. We will characterize conjugates spectroscopically, test photophysical properties, and evaluate specificity and phototoxicity in CSC-enriched spheroids and orthotopic mouse models of metastasis, comparing to non-targeted controls and nanoparticle-based combination therapies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, versatile platform technology for generating targeted PDT agents, decoupling photosensitizer optimization from ligand discovery. It will provide a systematic method to evaluate novel targeting strategies against CSCs and deliver proof-of-concept for a modular approach that could accelerate the development of precision phototherapies.
proposals.novelty: While PROTAC linkers are established for protein degradation, their adaptation to create a modular 'click-and-play' platform for PDT conjugates is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts targeted PDT development from bespoke chemical synthesis to a rapid, mix-and-match assembly of pre-validated modules.

proposals.title: Targeting Cancer Stem Cells in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer with CD44-Specific Peptide-Photosensitizer Conjugates for Enhanced Photodynamic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) are a major driver of tumor recurrence, metastasis, and therapy resistance, particularly in aggressive cancers like triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) often fails to eradicate these resilient subpopulations due to non-specific photosensitizer uptake and CSC defensive mechanisms. Developing targeted PDT agents that selectively accumulate in CSCs is a critical unmet need to improve long-term therapeutic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Conjugating a clinically relevant photosensitizer (e.g., Chlorin e6) to a novel, high-affinity peptide ligand for the CSC marker CD44 will result in a targeted agent that selectively internalizes in TNBC CSCs, leading to significantly enhanced photodynamic cytotoxicity against this population compared to non-targeted photosensitizers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a panel of conjugates using solid-phase peptide synthesis to create cyclic peptides targeting the CD44 receptor, based on pre-2026 phage display data. These will be covalently linked to Chlorin e6 via a cleavable cathepsin-B responsive linker. Conjugates will be characterized (HPLC, mass spec) and tested in vitro using CD44-high vs. CD44-low TNBC cell lines and patient-derived spheres. Specificity, internalization (confocal microscopy), and PDT efficacy (clonogenic and sphere-formation assays post-665nm laser) will be compared to free Ce6 and a non-targeted conjugate. A syngeneic 4T1 mouse model of TNBC will be used to assess in vivo targeting and control of metastasis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, rationally designed targeted PDT conjugate with validated specificity for CD44+ CSCs. It will demonstrate a proof-of-concept for a peptide-based targeting strategy to overcome CSC-mediated PDT resistance. The findings will contribute a new tool for combinatorial regimens, potentially integrating with PBMT or nanoparticle delivery systems to further enhance therapeutic index.
proposals.novelty: While antibody-PS conjugates exist, the use of novel, synthetically tunable peptide ligands for CSC-specific PDT targeting in TNBC is underexplored. The combination of a CD44-targeting peptide with a cathepsin-B cleavable linker is designed for precise intracellular activation specifically within CSCs, a strategy distinct from prior non-specific or antibody-based approaches.

proposals.title: Targeted Photodynamic Therapy of Cancer Stem Cells Using Ionizable Lipid Nanoparticles for Photosensitizer Delivery
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy is limited by the poor aqueous solubility and non-specific biodistribution of conventional photosensitizers (PS), leading to off-target effects and suboptimal tumor cell kill. This is particularly problematic for eradicating cancer stem cells (CSCs), which drive tumor recurrence and metastasis. A delivery platform that ensures targeted, efficient intracellular PS delivery is critically needed to advance PDT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ionizable lipid nanoparticles (LNPs), engineered with a tumor-targeting surface ligand and optimized for endosomal escape, will significantly enhance the delivery and photodynamic efficacy of hydrophobic photosensitizers against CSCs compared to free PS or conventional nanocarriers.
proposals.method: We will formulate LNPs using the ionizable lipid DLin-MC3-DMA (or analogous structures), cholesterol, DSPC, and PEG-lipid to encapsulate a model hydrophobic PS (e.g., Chlorin e6). LNPs will be surface-functionalized with an anti-CD44 antibody for CSC targeting. We will characterize LNP size, zeta potential, encapsulation efficiency, and photosensitizer release kinetics. In vitro, we will test cellular uptake, ROS generation, and phototoxicity in CD44+ breast CSCs versus control cells. In vivo, using a murine model of metastatic breast cancer, we will evaluate biodistribution, tumor accumulation, and the ability of LNP-PDT to inhibit primary tumor growth and lung metastasis compared to control groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, targeted LNP platform specifically designed for PS delivery, directly translating mRNA vaccine delivery advances to oncology. It will provide proof-of-concept that this strategy can overcome key pharmacokinetic barriers of PDT and demonstrate enhanced efficacy against therapeutically resistant CSCs, potentially impacting recurrence rates.
proposals.novelty: While LNPs are established for nucleic acids, their application for photosensitizer delivery is novel. The specific adaptation of ionizable lipid technology, proven for endosomal escape of mRNA, to achieve light-triggered cytosolic PS release and combined with CSC-targeting ligands, represents a significant and non-obvious innovation in PDT nanomedicine.

proposals.title: Mitochondria-Targeted Nanoparticle Conjugates for Subcellular Photodynamic Therapy Against Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis due to their resistance to conventional therapies. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hamblin's work, shows photodynamic therapy (PDT) and nanoparticle-based delivery are promising, but lack of subcellular precision limits efficacy and increases off-target damage. Organelle-specific targeting, particularly to mitochondria which regulate CSC apoptosis and metabolism, could overcome this limitation and enhance therapeutic selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating a clinically relevant photosensitizer (e.g., Chlorin e6) to mitochondria-targeting ligands (e.g., triphenylphosphonium, TPP) via a biodegradable polymeric nanoparticle platform will lead to specific accumulation in CSC mitochondria, and that subsequent photobiomodulation (PBMt) at a tailored wavelength will induce lethal, organelle-specific oxidative damage, eradicating CSCs with greater efficiency than non-targeted PDT.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize TPP-conjugated, curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4@Cur-TPP), building on our prior diabetic wound healing platform. CSCs from established breast cancer lines (MDA-MB-231) will be treated with these conjugates. Using confocal microscopy with organelle-specific fluorescent probes (e.g., MitoTracker), we will quantify subcellular localization. PDT will be administered using a polychromatic light device (as used in our antibacterial blood studies) at 660 nm. Outcomes will be assessed via assays for mitochondrial membrane potential (JC-1), ROS production (DCFH-DA), apoptosis (Annexin V/PI), and CSC sphere-forming capacity, comparing targeted vs. non-targeted nanoparticle groups and light-only controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, rationally designed nanoplatform for organelle-specific PDT, directly addressing CSC resistance. It will yield mechanistic insights into mitochondrial damage as a critical pathway for CSC elimination. The findings could establish a new paradigm for precision photomedicine, moving beyond cellular to subcellular targeting, with potential to improve outcomes in refractory cancers.
proposals.novelty: While organelle-targeting dyes exist, their integration into a therapeutic, PBMt-activated nanoparticle system validated against CSCs is novel. This proposal uniquely combines Dr. Hamblin's expertise in PBMt, nanoparticle-drug conjugates (e.g., curcumin-Fe3O4), and ROS-mediated therapies into a single, precision platform for subcellular oncology.

proposals.title: Systemic Photodynamic Therapy for Whole-Body Pathogen and Circulating Tumor Cell Clearance Using Targeted, Activatable Nanoparticles and Extracorporeal Illumination
proposals.motivation: Disseminated infections and hematogenous metastases remain major clinical challenges with limited treatment options. Systemic photodynamic therapy (PDT) offers a potential solution but is constrained by the lack of safe, effective photosensitizer (PS) delivery to circulating targets and the inability to deliver light systemically. Pre-2026 work on nanoparticle-based PS delivery and extracorporeal photopheresis provides a foundation for a scalable approach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a regimen combining intravenously administered, pathogen/cancer stem cell (CSC)-targeted nanoparticles with an activatable PS and extracorporeal blood illumination will safely and effectively eliminate circulating pathogens and tumor cells in vivo.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) conjugated with targeting ligands (e.g., antibodies against common pathogens or CSC markers) and loaded with a ruthenium-nitrosyl (Ru-NO) PS prodrug, building on our prior work with nitro ruthenium and curcumin-loaded IONPs. The Ru-NO complex releases cytotoxic nitric oxide (NO) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) only upon light activation. An extracorporeal circuit with a polychromatic light source (mimicking our blood irradiation studies) will be developed for a rodent model. We will test the regimen in two animal models: 1) a bacteremia model and 2) a model with circulating tumor cells, comparing nanoparticle + light, light-only, nanoparticle-only, and untreated control groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept platform for systemic PDT, demonstrating targeted cytotoxicity against circulating targets with minimized off-tissue effects. It will provide critical in vivo data on the safety and efficacy of combining targeted, activatable nanocarriers with extracorporeal illumination, advancing the field beyond localized PDT.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a targeted, dual-action (ROS/NO) activatable PS platform with an extracorporeal illumination strategy specifically designed for whole-body clearance, a significant scale-up from localized treatments or non-targeted blood irradiation.

proposals.title: Two-Photon Optimized Photosensitizer-Loaded Nanoparticles for Targeting and Eradicating Deep-Tissue Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is limited by the shallow penetration depth of visible light used to activate conventional photosensitizers (PS), hindering treatment of deep-seated or metastatic tumors.
