Two-photon absorption (2PA) using near-infrared (NIR) light enables deeper tissue penetration and highly localized activation, but existing PS have poor two-photon cross-sections. This project addresses the critical need for PS agents specifically engineered for efficient 2PA to target therapy-resistant cancer stem cells (CSCs) within deeper tissue layers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating a rationally designed, high two-photon cross-section photosensitizer (e.g., a porphyrin or bacteriochlorin derivative) to iron oxide nanoparticles will create a theranostic agent that, upon NIR two-photon excitation, will generate lethal reactive oxygen species (ROS) to selectively eradicate CSCs in deep 3D tumor models and inhibit metastasis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a library of PS candidates with donor-acceptor structures optimized for high 2PA cross-sections, guided by computational TD-DFT modeling. The lead PS will be loaded onto poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA)-coated iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) for magnetic targeting and imaging. Using in vitro 3D tumor spheroids enriched for CSCs (via flow-sorting), we will test efficacy with a femtosecond NIR laser (e.g., 800 nm) for two-photon activation. Control groups will include single-photon activation, nanoparticles alone, and free PS. Outcomes will be measured via ROS detection, CSC marker expression (CD44/CD133), spheroid disintegration, and invasion assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, targeted nanoplatform that merges photobiomodulation-adjacent principles with advanced photophysics for deep-tissue PDT. It will provide a proof-of-concept for using two-photon activation to overcome the depth limitation in treating CSCs, a major barrier in curative cancer therapy. The findings will establish a methodology for combining nanoparticle-mediated drug delivery with non-linear optical activation for precise oncological interventions.
proposals.novelty: While Hamblin's prior work combined PDT/Photobiomodulation with nanoparticles (e.g., curcumin-IONPs), the explicit optimization of PS for two-photon absorption and its application to target the deep-tissue CSC niche is a novel, non-obvious integration of non-linear optics with CSC biology and nanomedicine, moving beyond conventional single-photon activation paradigms.

proposals.title: Enzyme-Activatable Photosensitizer Prodrugs for Tumor-Selective Photodynamic Therapy Targeting Cancer Stem Cell Niches
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) is the reliance on selective photosensitizer (PS) accumulation in tumors, which is often imperfect and leads to off-target phototoxicity. This is particularly problematic for targeting cancer stem cells (CSCs), which drive metastasis and recurrence and reside in protective niches. Pre-2026 work, including Hamblin's on combined nanoparticles and photobiomodulation, highlights the need for smarter, more selective therapeutic agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PS prodrugs, designed to be activated specifically by tumor-associated enzymes (e.g., matrix metalloproteinases, cathepsins) overexpressed in the CSC niche, will generate cytotoxic singlet oxygen only within the diseased tissue, thereby improving therapeutic selectivity and reducing side effects regardless of the initial biodistribution.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel PS prodrugs by conjugating a core photosensitizer (e.g., chlorin e6) to a quenching moiety via a peptide linker cleavable by specific tumor-associated proteases. These constructs will be characterized spectroscopically. Activation kinetics and singlet oxygen yield post-cleavage will be measured in vitro. Efficacy will be tested in 3D co-culture spheroids containing CSCs, assessing cytotoxicity, CSC marker expression, and migratory capacity after light irradiation. A murine model of metastatic breast cancer will be used to evaluate biodistribution, tumor regression, metastasis control, and systemic safety compared to a non-activatable PS control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel chemical strategy to decouple PS delivery from PS activation, fundamentally relaxing the need for precise accumulation. It will deliver a new class of 'smart' PS agents with direct translational potential. Furthermore, it will advance the specific targeting of CSCs within their microenvironment, a critical step toward preventing recurrence and metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While activatable probes for imaging exist, their application in PDT, especially for targeting the CSC niche, is underexplored. The novelty lies in the rational design of the cleavable linker based on the protease profile of CSC niches and the integration of this approach with established PDT and nanoparticle expertise from the Hamblin lab.

proposals.title: Targeted Photodynamic Therapy of Cancer Stem Cells Using an Antibody-Photosensitizer Conjugate with a Tumor-Microenvironment-Responsive Linker
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy is limited by poor photosensitizer (PS) selectivity for tumor cells, leading to off-target effects and suboptimal damage to therapy-resistant cancer stem cells (CSCs). Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) technology has revolutionized targeted chemotherapy by enabling precise delivery of potent payloads, but this sophisticated targeting and linker chemistry has not been fully leveraged for PDT to improve PS specificity and activation control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating a clinically relevant PS to a monoclonal antibody targeting a CSC surface marker (e.g., CD44 or CD133) via a linker cleavable by tumor-microenvironment enzymes (e.g., matrix metalloproteinases) will result in a conjugate that selectively accumulates in CSCs and releases the active PS upon cleavage, leading to significantly enhanced phototoxicity against CSCs and reduced metastasis in vivo compared to non-targeted PS.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a conjugate using the anti-CD44 antibody and the PS Chlorin e6, linked by a peptide sequence cleavable by MMP-2/9. Conjugate characterization will include HPLC, spectroscopy, and in vitro stability assays. We will test specificity and phototoxicity in co-cultures of breast cancer CSCs and non-CSCs using flow cytometry and clonogenic assays. In vivo efficacy will be evaluated in an orthotopic mouse model of triple-negative breast cancer, comparing the conjugate to free PS and control IgG-PS conjugate. Primary endpoints will be primary tumor control, lung metastasis burden (histology), and CSC population reduction (flow cytometry of dissociated tumors).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for antibody-targeted PDT, directly applying ADC design principles to phototherapy. It will provide proof-of-concept that microenvironment-responsive linkers can enhance PS selectivity and activation within tumors, potentially overcoming a key limitation of conventional PDT. The project aims to deliver a targeted strategy specifically for eradicating CSCs, addressing a critical challenge in preventing cancer recurrence and metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While antibody-PS conjugates exist, the systematic application of advanced, conditionally cleavable ADC linker technology to control PS localization and activation specifically within the tumor microenvironment is novel. Furthermore, explicitly targeting this conjugate strategy against CSCs, a population implicated in treatment resistance and metastasis in Dr. Hamblin's recent work, represents a significant and non-obvious advancement over standard tumor-cell targeting.

proposals.title: In Silico Design of Tumor-Targeting Photosensitizers for Enhanced Photodynamic Therapy Against Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy is limited by suboptimal photosensitizers (PSs) with poor selectivity for cancer stem cells (CSCs), a key driver of tumor recurrence and metastasis. Current PS development relies on costly and slow empirical screening. Computational drug design, widely used in pharmaceutical development pre-2026, offers a powerful, rational approach to optimize PS properties, but its application to PDT, particularly for targeting CSCs, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structure-based and ligand-based computational modeling can be used to design novel PS molecules with optimized photophysical properties and enhanced binding affinity for CSC-specific molecular targets, leading to improved PDT efficacy.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step in silico pipeline. First, molecular docking (using AutoDock Vina) and molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS) will model PS interactions with known CSC surface markers (e.g., CD44, CD133). Second, quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models will be built using machine learning (e.g., random forest or support vector machines) on existing PS libraries to predict critical properties like singlet oxygen quantum yield and lipophilicity. Finally, virtual screening of large chemical libraries (e.g., ZINC15) will identify top candidate structures, which will be synthesized and tested in vitro on CSC-enriched tumor spheroids.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational framework for the rational design of next-generation PSs. It is expected to yield new PS candidates with demonstrably higher specificity for CSCs, directly addressing the challenge of tumor relapse. The project will bridge the fields of computational pharmacology and photobiomodulation/PDT, providing a new methodology for the field.
proposals.novelty: While computational design is standard in drug discovery, its targeted application to photosensitizer optimization for CSC-specific PDT is novel. The integration of molecular dynamics for PS-target interaction with QSAR for photochemical property prediction represents a unique, multi-parameter design strategy beyond simple screening.

proposals.title: Targeting Cancer Stem Cells with Light-Triggered, Sustained-Release Polymeric Nanoparticles for Enhanced Photodynamic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) are a major driver of tumor recurrence and metastasis due to their resistance to conventional therapies. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is promising but limited by poor photosensitizer (PS) bioavailability, rapid clearance, and an inability to maintain therapeutic concentrations to eradicate resilient CSCs. Pre-2026, advanced drug delivery systems for chemotherapy have shown success in improving pharmacokinetics, but their application to PDT, especially for CSC targeting, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating a clinically relevant PS (e.g., Verteporfin or a porphyrin derivative) within a light-triggered, sustained-release polymeric nanoparticle system will enhance PS accumulation in CSCs, provide controlled ROS generation upon irradiation, and significantly improve anti-tumor efficacy by overcoming CSC-mediated therapy resistance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles loaded with the PS, optimizing for size (<200 nm) and surface charge for enhanced permeability and retention (EPR). Nanoparticles will be characterized for encapsulation efficiency, singlet oxygen yield, and in vitro release kinetics (sustained vs. light-triggered). Using established breast cancer cell lines enriched for CSCs (via mammosphere assays), we will test cellular uptake, dark toxicity, and PDT efficacy (using a 670 nm diode laser, similar to pre-2026 PBMT setups). Treatment groups will include free PS, PS-loaded nanoparticles with/without light, and controls. Metastatic potential will be assessed via invasion assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel PDT platform that merges controlled-release drug delivery principles with photodynamic activation, specifically designed to target CSCs. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on how nanoparticle-mediated PS delivery alters the dynamics of ROS production and cytotoxicity in therapy-resistant cell populations, potentially leading to more durable treatment responses.
proposals.novelty: While polymeric nanoparticles are established for chemotherapy, their use for *controlled, light-triggered release* of a PS to specifically target and overcome CSC resistance in PDT is a novel combinatorial strategy. This approach directly extends Dr. Hamblin's recent work on combined PBMT/nanoparticles and metastasis into a targeted, stimulus-responsive therapeutic paradigm.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Precision Photodynamic Therapy Using Pathogen-Targeted Nanoparticles for Eradication of Intracellular Bacteria
proposals.motivation: Current antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) lacks cellular precision, damaging host tissue and failing to effectively target intracellular pathogens, a major cause of persistent infections. The rise of antibiotic resistance necessitates novel, localized antimicrobial strategies. Dr. Hamblin's recent work combining photobiomodulation (PBM) and curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles for wound healing demonstrates a platform that can be adapted for ultra-targeted pathogen killing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating a highly specific photosensitizer (PS) to nanoparticles functionalized with antibodies or aptamers against a model intracellular pathogen (e.g., *Staphylococcus aureus*) will enable selective PS internalization into infected host cells and subsequent light activation to eradicate the pathogen at the single-cell level while sparing neighboring uninfected cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) as a core, leveraging Dr. Hamblin's prior experience. These will be conjugated with a potent, pre-2026 PS (e.g., chlorin e6) and surface-functionalized with anti-*S. aureus* antibodies. In vitro, macrophages will be infected with GFP-expressing *S. aureus*. Using confocal microscopy, we will quantify nanoparticle binding specificity and PS colocalization with intracellular bacteria. A control group will receive non-targeted PS-nanoparticles. Precision light delivery will be achieved using a focused 660 nm laser via a microscope-coupled system, enabling irradiation of single infected cells. Cell viability (host and bacterial) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation will be assayed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for spatially precise, single-cell aPDT, directly addressing the limitation of off-target damage. It will contribute a novel nanoparticle design paradigm combining immunological targeting with photodynamic activation for intracellular infections. The findings could translate to a platform technology for treating localized, persistent bacterial infections without systemic antibiotic use.
proposals.novelty: While nanoparticle-PDT combinations exist, the novelty lies in the integration of pathogen-specific targeting for single-cell, intracellular precision killing, moving beyond tissue-level treatment. This approach uniquely combines Hamblin's expertise in PBM/PDT, nanoparticle-mediated delivery, and controlled, localized therapy to achieve an unprecedented scale of selectivity in antimicrobial phototherapy.

proposals.title: Development of a Whole-Body Photodynamic Therapy System Using Uniform LED Arrays and Photosensitizer-Loaded Nanoparticles for the Treatment of Psoriasis
proposals.motivation: Psoriasis is a chronic, debilitating skin disease affecting millions, and conventional phototherapy (e.g., narrowband UVB) often lacks selectivity and can cause adverse effects. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) offers a targeted approach but is limited to treating small, localized areas due to challenges in delivering uniform light and photosensitizer to large, contoured surfaces. Scaling PDT to safely and effectively treat extensive body surface areas remains a significant unmet clinical need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a whole-body light delivery system utilizing a calibrated array of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) combined with a topically applied, nanoparticle-encapsulated photosensitizer will achieve uniform, therapeutically effective fluence across the entire body surface, leading to superior clearance of psoriatic plaques compared to conventional UVB.
proposals.method: We will engineer a whole-body light chamber with multiple, independently controlled LED panels (630 nm ± 10 nm) and integrated optical sensors to map and correct for fluence irregularities in real-time. The photosensitizer, a known PS like 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) or a curcumin analog, will be encapsulated in iron oxide nanoparticles (as per Hamblin's prior work) to enhance skin penetration and selectivity. In a murine model of psoriasis (imiquimod-induced), animals will be randomized into control, LED-only, nanoparticle-only, and combined treatment groups. Treatment efficacy will be assessed through histological scoring, measurement of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and bioluminescent imaging of reactive oxygen species production.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a scalable, uniform whole-body PDT platform. It will generate critical data on the safety and synergistic efficacy of combining advanced light delivery with nanoparticle-based photosensitizer formulations for a major inflammatory skin disease. The findings could establish a new paradigm for treating extensive cutaneous conditions beyond psoriasis.
proposals.novelty: While PDT and nanoparticles have been studied separately, the integration of a real-time fluence-corrected, whole-body LED array with a topically delivered nanoparticle-photosensitizer complex for systemic surface treatment is novel. This approach directly addresses the critical engineering and pharmacological barriers that have prevented the scaling of PDT.

proposals.title: Selective Cytoprotection of Normal Tissue via Low-Dose Photodynamic Priming: A Strategy to Mitigate Chemotherapy-Induced Oral Mucositis
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of cytotoxic cancer therapies like chemotherapy is severe damage to rapidly dividing healthy tissues, such as the oral mucosa, leading to painful and dose-limiting mucositis. Pre-2026 research by Hamblin and others has established that photobiomodulation (PBM) and certain photodynamic therapy (PDT) parameters can induce cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory effects. This proposal inverts the traditional cytotoxic application of PDT to proactively protect healthy cells, addressing a critical unmet need in oncology supportive care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-treatment with a very low-dose, non-cytotoxic PDT protocol, using a clinically approved photosensitizer at a fraction of the therapeutic dose, will activate endogenous antioxidant and pro-survival pathways in normal oral keratinocytes, thereby increasing their resilience to subsequent chemotherapy-induced damage without protecting cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will use in vitro models of human oral keratinocytes (OKF6/TERT-2) and head and neck squamous cell carcinoma cells. Cells will be primed with low-dose 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA)-induced PpIX and 635 nm light at fluences known from pre-2026 work to be sub-cytotoxic (e.g., 0.5-2 J/cm²). Primed cells will then be challenged with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU). Key outcomes will include cell viability (MTT assay), intracellular ROS (DCFH-DA assay), and activation of Nrf2/ARE and HSF-1/HSP pathways (western blot). A parallel in vivo study in a rat model of 5-FU-induced oral mucositis will validate findings, using a dedicated PBM/PDT device for oral cavity illumination.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, mechanism-driven protocol for 'photodynamic cytoprotection,' directly translating PDT/PBM molecular knowledge into a practical supportive therapy. It will provide pre-clinical proof-of-concept for using light-based interventions to widen the therapeutic window of chemotherapy by shielding normal tissue, potentially reducing morbidity and improving treatment adherence.
proposals.novelty: While PDT is exclusively used for ablation and PBM for healing, this study uniquely proposes a hybrid, pre-conditioning strategy that co-opts the precise, photosensitizer-dependent initiation of PDT to trigger a calibrated protective response, a deliberate inversion of its classic mechanism not explored in existing pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dose-Dependent Biphasic Modulation of Reactive Oxygen Species by Photobiomodulation: A Mechanistic Study in Cancer Stem Cells and Normal Fibroblasts
proposals.motivation: Photobiomodulation (PBM) therapy, historically known as low-level laser therapy (LLLT), exhibits a well-documented biphasic dose response where low fluences stimulate cellular processes while high fluences inhibit them. The precise role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in mediating these opposing effects remains unclear, particularly in the context of cancer therapy where differential effects on cancer stem cells (CSCs) and normal cells are critical. Clarifying this ROS-mediated mechanism is essential for optimizing PBM parameters to selectively target CSCs while protecting normal tissue, thereby improving its safety and efficacy as an adjuvant cancer treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-fluence PBM induces a transient, sub-toxic increase in mitochondrial ROS that activates cytoprotective and proliferative signaling pathways (e.g., Nrf2, Akt), whereas high-fluence PBM generates a sustained, supra-physiological ROS burst that leads to oxidative damage, inhibition of proliferation, and induction of cell death, with CSCs exhibiting a distinct ROS threshold and response profile compared to normal cells.
proposals.method: We will use in vitro models of breast cancer stem cells (CD44+/CD24- sorted from MCF-7 line) and normal human dermal fibroblasts. Cells will be irradiated with a 660nm laser at fluences ranging from 0.5 J/cm² (low) to 64 J/cm² (high). Intracellular ROS will be quantified in real-time using the fluorescent probe H2DCFDA and by measuring mitochondrial superoxide with MitoSOX Red. Specific ROS types (H2O2, O2•−, •OH) will be identified using selective scavengers (e.g., N-acetylcysteine, PEG-catalase). Downstream signaling will be analyzed via western blot for phospho-Akt, Nrf2, and NF-κB at multiple time points post-irradiation. Functional outcomes (viability, clonogenicity, migration) will be correlated with ROS profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed, mechanistic map linking specific PBM doses to quantifiable ROS signatures and their divergent biological consequences. It will directly inform the development of precise PBM protocols aimed at exploiting the differential ROS stress thresholds between CSCs and normal cells. The findings will strengthen the theoretical foundation for using PBM as a targeted, ROS-mediated modality in oncology, particularly for mitigating metastasis and treatment resistance.
proposals.novelty: While the biphasic dose response is known, systematically correlating a continuum of light doses with specific ROS species kinetics and downstream pathway activation in a paired CSC-normal cell model is novel. This approach moves beyond observing net ROS levels to defining the 'ROS signature' (type, magnitude, duration) that dictates therapeutic versus inhibitory outcomes.

proposals.title: Sequential High- and Low-Dose Photobiomodulation to Modulate Cancer Stem Cell Dynamics and Metastatic Potential in a Preclinical Model
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, presenting a major therapeutic challenge. Photobiomodulation (PBMt) exhibits a biphasic dose-response, where high fluences can inhibit cell proliferation and low fluences can stimulate repair. This duality has not been strategically exploited to target the CSC niche within a tumor, where selective inhibition of CSCs followed by stimulation of normal tissue repair could improve outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential PBMt protocol—first applying a high, inhibitory light dose to target CSCs and disrupt the metastatic niche, followed by a low, stimulatory dose—will selectively impair CSC viability and metastatic capacity while promoting the healing of surrounding normal stromal tissue.
proposals.method: We will use a murine model of breast cancer metastasis. Tumors will be irradiated in vivo using a 660nm laser. The experimental group will receive a sequential treatment: Day 1, a high fluence (e.g., 100 J/cm², 100 mW/cm²) designed to generate cytotoxic levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS); Day 2, a low fluence (e.g., 5 J/cm², 20 mW/cm²) aimed at stimulating mitochondrial function and tissue repair. Control groups will receive sham, high-dose only, or low-dose only treatments. Primary tumor growth, lung metastasis (histology), and CSC markers (CD44+/CD24- via flow cytometry) will be quantified. Nanoparticle-enhanced PBMt, based on our prior work with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles, will be tested as an adjunct to increase specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, non-invasive therapeutic paradigm that leverages the biphasic nature of PBMt for cancer management. It will provide direct evidence on whether sequential dosing can decouple detrimental pro-tumor effects from beneficial healing effects, a critical concern in oncology PBMt. The findings could lead to a refined clinical protocol for post-surgical adjuvant PBMt to prevent recurrence.
proposals.novelty: While the biphasic dose-response is known, the intentional, timed sequencing of high-inhibitory and low-stimulatory doses to spatially and functionally separate effects on CSCs versus normal tissue is a novel and untested strategy. Combining this with nanoparticle sensitization, as suggested by our group's recent pre-2026 work, further enhances the specificity of the approach.

proposals.title: Neutrally Charged Iron Oxide Nanocomposites for Photodynamic Bacterial Eradication: A Mechanistic Study on Non-Electrostatic Killing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on antibacterial nanoparticles heavily favors cationic (positively charged) designs for membrane disruption, but this can cause cytotoxicity and rapid opsonization in vivo. Dr. Hamblin's recent work combining photobiomodulation (PBM) and curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles for wound healing suggests a potent, light-activated platform where surface charge requirements may differ. Exploring non-cationic nanocomposites could unlock safer, more selective antimicrobial strategies with better in vivo compatibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neutrally or negatively charged iron oxide nanocomposites, when functionalized with a photosensitizer like curcumin and activated by specific PBM wavelengths, can effectively kill bacteria through photodynamic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and other non-electrostatic mechanisms, independent of direct membrane disruption via electrostatic attraction.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize three groups of iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs): positively charged (amine-coated, control), neutrally charged (PEG-coated), and negatively charged (carboxylate-coated). All groups will be loaded with curcumin. Using standard microbiological assays, we will compare their antibacterial efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli, with and without exposure to a 660nm PBM light source (parameters from Hamblin's prior work). We will quantify ROS production (using probes like DCFH-DA), bacterial membrane integrity (via LIVE/DEAD staining and SEM), and nanoparticle internalization (using TEM and fluorescence microscopy) to delineate the killing mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for effective, charge-neutral antimicrobial nanocomposites, expanding the design principles beyond electrostatic binding. It will directly contribute to Dr. Hamblin's photobiomodulation-therapeutic nanoparticle platform by defining the charge-dependence of photodynamic killing, potentially leading to next-generation wound dressings with improved biocompatibility. The findings will challenge the prevailing paradigm that high positive charge is essential for nanomaterial-based antibacterial activity.
proposals.novelty: While cationic nanoparticles are standard, systematically testing neutrally/negatively charged IONP- photosensitizer conjugates under PBM for antibacterial action is novel. The approach leverages Hamblin's combined PBM-nanoparticle expertise to decouple photodynamic mechanisms from electrostatic effects, a non-obvious direction given the field's focus on charge-driven membrane lysis.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Decoupling: Evaluating Physical Membrane Disruption as a Primary Cytotoxic Pathway of Photoactivated Nanocomposites in Cancer Stem Cells, Independent of Reactive Oxygen Species
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Hamblin's work, predominantly attributes the anticancer effects of photobiomodulation (PBM) and photoactivated nanoparticles to reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. However, this assumption may overlook significant physical mechanisms of action, such as direct membrane disruption by nanostructures, which could be crucial for targeting therapy-resistant cancer stem cells (CSCs) where ROS defenses are elevated. Clarifying this mechanism is essential for rationally designing more effective combinatorial nanotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific photoactivated nanocomposites (e.g., curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles) induce significant physical disruption of cancer stem cell membranes, leading to cytotoxicity independently of canonical ROS-mediated signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (C-IONPs) as per our prior work. Using established CSC models (e.g., breast cancer MDA-MB-231 spheres), we will treat cells with C-IONPs and 810 nm laser irradiation under strict ROS-inhibition using a cocktail of scavengers (NAC, Catalase, SOD) and by performing experiments in anoxic conditions. Membrane integrity will be assessed in real-time via live-cell imaging with propidium iodide and FM dyes, complemented by high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to visualize physical membrane lesions. Cytotoxicity will be quantified via clonogenic assays and compared to control groups with active ROS generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for a ROS-independent, physical mechanism of photodynamic nanocomposite action, challenging the prevailing paradigm. It will establish a foundational rationale for designing next-generation nanoparticles that optimize membrane-targeting properties, potentially overcoming CSC resistance mechanisms linked to ROS scavenging.
proposals.novelty: While ROS is widely assumed to be the primary kill mechanism, systematically isolating and proving a significant physical disruption effect in the presence of comprehensive ROS blockade is novel. This approach directly tests a core, untested assumption in the photobiomodulation and nanomedicine field.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Elucidation of Reactive Oxygen Species-Mediated Bacterial Killing by Photobiomodulation-Enhanced Curcumin-Iron Oxide Nanocomposites
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance necessitates novel, non-antibiotic therapies. Photobiomodulation (PBM) combined with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (Cur-IONPs) shows synergistic antibacterial effects, but the precise oxidative damage pathways and primary cellular targets (e.g., membrane lipids, DNA, proteins) remain undefined. A detailed mechanistic understanding is critical for optimizing these nanocomposites for clinical translation against resistant infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic antibacterial effect of PBM and Cur-IONPs is due to a specific, amplified generation of hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen, which preferentially target bacterial membrane integrity and essential metabolic enzymes, leading to rapid, cascading cell death.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize Cur-IONPs as per our prior work. Using specific ROS scavengers (e.g., sodium azide for singlet oxygen, mannitol for hydroxyl radicals) and fluorescent probes (DCFH-DA, SOSG), we will quantify the type and flux of ROS produced under 660nm/810nm PBM. We will employ transmission electron microscopy and membrane potential dyes to assess primary physical damage. Proteomic and lipidomic analysis of treated vs. control bacterial cells (S. aureus, E. coli) will identify the specific oxidative modifications to proteins and lipids, pinpointing the critical lethal targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond phenomenological observation to provide a molecular-level map of the oxidative damage cascade. It will identify the key vulnerable nodes in bacterial physiology targeted by this combinatorial therapy, offering rational design principles for next-generation antimicrobial nano-agents. The findings will significantly advance the fundamental science of light-activated nanotherapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While PBM and curcumin are individually studied, the systematic deconvolution of the specific ROS species and their biomolecular targets within a synergistic PBM-nanoparticle system is novel. Integrating real-time ROS quantification with comprehensive oxidative lipidomics/proteomics in this context represents a significant methodological advance over existing studies.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Analysis of Bacterial Heterogeneity in Response to Photobiomodulation-Enhanced Nanocomposite Therapy Using Droplet Microfluidics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from Dr. Hamblin's group, shows that combined photobiomodulation (PBM) and nanocomposite treatments (e.g., curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles) can significantly enhance antibacterial and anti-biofilm effects. However, population-level assays mask critical single-cell heterogeneity in bacterial response, which can drive treatment failure and recurrence. Understanding this heterogeneity is crucial for optimizing combinatorial antimicrobial therapies against resilient infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that droplet microfluidics will reveal distinct subpopulations of bacteria with differential susceptibility to PBM-nanocomposite treatment, and that this heterogeneity is driven by variations in reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and uptake of nanocomposites at the single-cell level.
proposals.method: We will employ a pressure-driven flow-focusing droplet microfluidic device to encapsulate single GFP-expressing *Staphylococcus aureus* cells with precise doses of curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles in picoliter aqueous droplets within a fluorinated oil carrier. Droplets will be arrayed and irradiated using a 660nm or 810nm laser diode (parameters based on Hamblin's prior PBM work). Single-cell viability (via fluorescence loss) and ROS production (using a cell-permeable ROS sensor) will be tracked in real-time using high-speed fluorescence microscopy. Data will be analyzed using custom algorithms for droplet tracking and single-cell fluorescence kinetics to classify response phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-throughput, single-cell resolution map of bacterial phenotypic heterogeneity in response to a combined PBM-nanocomposite therapy. It will identify specific subpopulations responsible for survival, directly linking survival mechanisms to quantifiable parameters like nanocomposite load and ROS burst. This will establish a foundational methodology for precision optimization of antimicrobial photodynamic and photobiomodulation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for single-bacterium antibiotic studies, its application to dissect the mechanism of light-activated nanocomposite therapies is novel. The integration of real-time ROS sensing within droplets during PBM irradiation represents a significant technical advance over endpoint bulk assays.

proposals.title: Enzyme-Responsive Nanoparticles for Spatiotemporally Controlled Reactive Oxygen Species Generation in Photodynamic Therapy of Cancer Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Cancer stem cells (CSCs) drive tumor recurrence and metastasis, and their microenvironment often exhibits unique enzymatic signatures, such as elevated matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs). Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Hamblin's on combined photobiomodulation (PBM) and nanoparticles, shows promise but lacks specificity. A major challenge in photodynamic therapy (PDT) is the off-target effects of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can damage healthy tissue and limit therapeutic efficacy against resistant CSCs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering photosensitizer-loaded nanoparticles with MMP-9 cleavable peptide linkers will enable tumor microenvironment-triggered ROS release, specifically targeting CSCs and minimizing off-target cytotoxicity, thereby enhancing the anti-metastatic effect of PDT.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) coated with a silica shell, conjugating the photosensitizer Chlorin e6 via an MMP-9 sensitive peptide linker (GPLGVRGK). Control groups will use a non-cleavable linker. Nanoparticles will be characterized for size, zeta potential, and photosensitizer loading. In vitro, CSC-enriched populations from MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells will be treated with nanoparticles ± laser irradiation (660 nm). ROS generation, nanoparticle uptake, and linker cleavage will be assayed using DCFH-DA, fluorescence microscopy, and HPLC. Efficacy will be evaluated via clonogenic assays, CSC marker expression (CD44+/CD24-), and inhibition of migration/invasion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for enzyme-activated PDT nanoplatforms, directly addressing the challenge of spatial control in ROS therapies. It will provide novel data on targeting CSCs within their niche using a stimuli-responsive material, potentially improving the therapeutic index. The findings could translate to a more precise combinatorial strategy against metastatic disease.
proposals.novelty: While stimuli-responsive drug delivery is established, its application to control the release of ROS *in situ* for PDT, specifically targeting the enzymatic hallmarks of the CSC niche, is a novel integration of materials science and photonic therapy. This moves beyond simple combination therapies to a biologically targeted, activation-on-demand system.

proposals.title: Bacteria-Responsive Nanocomposites for Photodynamic Therapy: Enzyme-Triggered Release of Nitric Oxide and Curcumin to Eradicate Biofilms
proposals.motivation: Chronic wound infections and cancer therapy-related biofilms are often resistant to conventional antibiotics and photodynamic therapy (PDT) due to poor drug penetration and bacterial enzyme activity. Pre-2026 work, including Hamblin's own, shows the promise of combining photobiomodulation (PBM) with nanoparticle-delivered agents like curcumin, and the bactericidal role of reactive oxygen/nitrogen species. A strategy that turns bacterial defense mechanisms against themselves could overcome treatment resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing a nanocomposite whose polymeric shell is degraded by specific bacterial enzymes (e.g., lipase, hyaluronidase) will trigger the localized release of a dual payload—a nitric oxide (NO) donor and curcumin—leading to a synergistic, bacteria-triggered antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) effect significantly more potent than either agent or light alone.
proposals.method: We will synthesize iron oxide nanoparticles (core) co-loaded with a ruthenium nitrosyl complex (NO donor) and curcumin, encapsulated in a bacteria-degradable polymer (e.g., poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) or a hyaluronic acid derivative). In vitro, we will test degradation and payload release kinetics in the presence of bacterial cultures vs. controls. The antibacterial and anti-biofilm efficacy will be assessed against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa using colony-forming unit counts and confocal microscopy, comparing groups: nanoparticles alone, nanoparticles + enzyme, nanoparticles + enzyme + red/NIR light (for PBM-triggered NO release and curcumin activation).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for 'smart' aPDT nanocomposites that are activated specifically in infected microenvironments, potentially reducing off-target effects. It will provide a novel combinatorial strategy (enzyme-triggered release + PBM) to enhance the penetration and potency of antimicrobials, directly contributing to the fields of infectious disease control and adjuvant cancer therapy (e.g., for oral mucositis).
proposals.novelty: While enzyme-responsive drug delivery and combinatorial PDT exist, the integration of a bacterial enzyme as the primary trigger for releasing a dual payload (NO + curcumin) designed for subsequent photo-activation is novel. This creates a two-stage antimicrobial response where the bacteria initiate their own targeted, more potent destruction.

proposals.title: Synergistic Immunogenic Cell Death and T-cell Priming: Combining Verteporfin-PDT with Adoptive T-cell Therapy for Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is known to induce immunogenic cell death (ICD), releasing tumor antigens and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) that can prime anti-tumor immunity. However, this endogenous immune response is often insufficient to control metastatic disease. Adoptive T-cell therapy (ACT) provides potent effector cells but struggles with solid tumor infiltration and immunosuppressive microenvironments. Combining PDT's in situ vaccination effect with ACT could overcome these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-conditioning a tumor with PDT will create a pro-inflammatory, antigen-rich microenvironment that will enhance the homing, persistence, and cytotoxic efficacy of systemically administered tumor-specific T cells, leading to superior primary tumor control and inhibition of metastasis.
proposals.method: We will use a syngeneic 4T1 murine model of metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Tumors will be treated with verteporfin-mediated PDT (670 nm, 50 J/cm²). 24 hours post-PDT, mice will receive an intravenous infusion of in vitro expanded, 4T1-specific CD8+ T cells (control groups: PDT alone, ACT alone, sham). Tumor growth and lung metastasis will be monitored. We will analyze the tumor immune microenvironment by flow cytometry (for T-cell infiltration, activation markers, and myeloid-derived suppressor cells) and cytokine profiling. Nanoparticle-based imaging agents (pre-2026 iron oxide or silica platforms) may be used to track T-cell homing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide mechanistic evidence for PDT as an effective local adjuvant for cellular immunotherapies. It will define the optimal timing and immune context for combining ICD-inducing therapies with ACT. The findings could directly inform clinical trial design for treating locally advanced or oligometastatic cancers.
proposals.novelty: While PDT's immune-stimulating effects are known, its systematic use to precondition the tumor bed specifically for enhancing adoptive T-cell therapy is underexplored. This proposal integrates Hamblin's expertise in PDT and nanoparticle-mediated delivery with the immunological goal of improving ACT efficacy, a novel combinatorial strategy.

proposals.title: Photodynamic Therapy-Generated Tumor Antigen Enhances Dendritic Cell Vaccine Efficacy Against Cancer Stem Cells and Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is known to induce immunogenic cell death (ICD), releasing tumor-associated antigens and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). However, its potential to generate a targeted, ex vivo dendritic cell (DC) vaccine, particularly against therapy-resistant cancer stem cells (CSCs) that drive metastasis, is underexplored. Combining PDT's immunogenic potential with nanoparticle-enhanced antigen capture, as suggested by Hamblin's prior work on combination therapies, could address the critical need for vaccines that induce systemic, anti-metastatic immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lysates from PDT-treated tumor cells, especially when combined with curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles to modulate the tumor microenvironment, will provide a superior antigen source for ex vivo DC loading, generating a vaccine that elicits a potent T-cell response against CSCs and inhibits metastatic spread more effectively than lysates from cells killed by other means.
proposals.method: We will establish a murine 4T1 breast cancer model. Tumors will be treated in vivo with methylene blue-mediated PDT, with a control group receiving PDT combined with intratumoral curcumin-loaded iron oxide nanoparticles (based on Hamblin's prior formulation). Twenty-four hours post-treatment, tumors will be resected, and single-cell suspensions prepared. Lysates from these cells will be used to pulse bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDCs) ex vivo. These antigen-loaded DCs will be used to vaccinate syngeneic mice, followed by challenge with live 4T1 cells. Primary tumor growth, lung metastasis burden (histology), and circulating CSC markers (CD44+/CD24- by flow cytometry) will be quantified. Immune profiling of splenic T-cells will assess tumor-specific cytotoxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel protocol for generating potent anti-tumor DC vaccines using the immunogenic payload created by PDT in situ. It will provide direct pre-clinical evidence on the ability of a PDT-based vaccine to target CSCs and suppress metastasis. The findings could translate into a synergistic clinical strategy where a primary PDT session serves both for debulking and as an 'antigen harvest' for adjuvant autologous vaccination.
proposals.novelty: While PDT is known to be immunogenic, its systematic use as an in situ antigen source for ex vivo DC vaccine production is novel. The combination with nanoparticle-preconditioned tumors to potentially enrich for CSC antigens and enhance DAMP release creates a unique, biomodulation-enhanced vaccination strategy beyond simple cell lysate vaccines.

ideas.title: Adapting mRNA Vaccine Lipid Nanoparticle Technology for Photosensitizer Delivery
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from the field of mRNA Vaccine Development, where the delivery challenge of fragile nucleic acids was solved using engineered ionizable lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). The analogous solution involves formulating LNPs with specific lipid compositions to protect the payload, ensure cellular uptake, and enable endosomal escape. Adapted to the original research context, the idea is to design and test novel LNP carriers specifically for photosensitizers. By leveraging the ionizable lipid technology proven in vaccines, we can encapsulate hydrophobic photosensitizers, conjugate tumor-targeting ligands to the LNP surface, and engineer the lipids for stability in circulation and controlled release upon light exposure. This approach aims to solve the key problems of poor solubility, non-specific biodistribution, and inefficient cellular uptake that currently limit photodynamic therapy.

ideas.title: Create targeted PDT conjugates using a modular, clickable linker system adapted from PROTAC technology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the modular, heterobifunctional solution from the field of targeted protein degradation (PROTACs) to the challenge of creating targeted PDT conjugates. In PROTACs, a target protein ligand and an E3 ligase recruiter are connected by a flexible linker to form a degradation complex. Analogously, we propose developing a platform technology for PDT where a photosensitizer core is functionalized with a standardized, bioorthogonal linker. This linker system would allow for the rapid, modular conjugation of a wide array of novel antibody, peptide, or protein ligands from a pre-synthesized library. This approach separates the optimization of the photophysical properties of the photosensitizer from the biological targeting step, enabling faster, more systematic creation of receptor-specific PDT agents by simply mixing and matching compatible modules.

ideas.title: Establish a chitosan reference material system and validated assay cascade
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the chitosan standardization problem by adapting the proven solution from the biopharmaceutical field for complex macromolecular therapeutics. The analogous problem—the need for global comparability of biological activity and quality for proteins like cytokines and antibodies—was solved through the creation of international physical reference standards (like WHO International Standards) and a defined hierarchy of validated analytical and biological assays. Adapted to chitosan, the research would involve: 1) Producing and certifying a panel of chitosan reference materials that represent key source and processing variables. 2) Developing and validating a mandatory testing protocol cascade, moving from basic physicochemical identity tests (e.g., standardized SEC-MALS for molecular weight, NMR for DA/PA) to a functional biochemical assay (e.g., a standardized fluorescent probe binding assay for polycationic activity), and culminating in a standardized in vitro bioassay (e.g., using a defined immune cell line to measure a specific response). This system would allow any chitosan batch to be benchmarked against the reference materials and have its activity reported in standardized units, enabling true reproducibility and comparison across studies and commercial products.

ideas.title: Chitosan Nanofiber Patches with Hierarchical Porosity via Ice-Templating
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from materials science for energy storage—specifically, the creation of hierarchical porous electrodes via ice-templating—to the biomedical context of wound patches. The analogous problem of maximizing reactive surface area and transport pathways is solved in batteries by building dual-scale porous architectures. The adapted research idea is to fabricate chitosan nanofiber mats via electrospinning and then use ice-templating on this scaffold. This hybrid process will create a wound patch with the inherent nano-scale features for enhanced cell interaction, plus an engineered network of micro-channels to facilitate rapid drainage of exudate, improved oxygen diffusion, and guided cell infiltration into the wound bed, addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension by integrating nano- and micro-structural control.

ideas.title: Dual-Lock Photosensitizers Activated by Target Binding and Microenvironment Cues
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates photosensitizers that remain completely inert until two distinct conditions are met simultaneously: 1) specific binding to the intended molecular target, and 2) detection of a second, disease-specific microenvironmental factor (e.g., an overexpressed enzyme or pathological pH). The photosensitizer's structure incorporates two separate quenching/protecting groups, each removed or reconfigured by one of the two triggers. This AND-gate logic ensures activation is confined almost exclusively to the intended site, providing a robust safeguard against off-target binding artifacts and vastly improving therapeutic specificity.

ideas.title: Establish a predictive structure-function database to define functional chitosan subclasses for targeted applications
ideas.description: This project moves beyond one-size-fits-all standardization by first building a foundational understanding of chitosan's structure-function relationships. It systematically generates a diverse library of chitosan variants from different sources and processing methods, characterizing each with a suite of advanced analytical techniques to create a detailed physicochemical fingerprint. These variants are then tested in a panel of standardized biological assays relevant to key applications (e.g., antimicrobial, wound healing, drug delivery). Using machine learning, the data is integrated into an open-access predictive model and database that links material fingerprints to specific functionalities, enabling the rational definition of performance-based subclasses and the subsequent development of fit-for-purpose standards.

ideas.title: Adapting Continuous Flow Biopharmaceutical Purification for Scalable Chitosan Production
ideas.description: This research idea applies the scaling solution from biopharmaceutical manufacturing—specifically, the development of integrated continuous bioprocessing (ICB) for monoclonal antibodies—to the challenge of mass-producing standardized chitosan. The analogous problem was the need to move from expensive, variable batch purification to cost-effective, consistent continuous production of a high-purity biological polymer. The analogous solution was multi-column countercurrent chromatography with inline monitoring and control. Adapted to chitosan, the idea is to develop a cost-effective, scalable industrial process by designing a continuous, modular purification system. This system would integrate continuous deacetylation, filtration, and purification steps, using real-time sensors and automated control loops to maintain strict specifications for degree of deacetylation, molecular weight, and purity, thereby enabling reliable mass production of standardized, medical-grade chitosan.

ideas.title: Adapting adaptive platform trial design from oncology to chitosan burn dressing trials
ideas.description: This idea adapts the adaptive platform trial design, a solution pioneered in oncology drug development (e.g., the I-SPY 2 trial), to the clinical validation of chitosan burn dressings. The analogous problem in oncology involves efficiently testing multiple novel therapies for a complex disease (cancer) with heterogeneous subtypes. The solution was a master protocol with a shared control group, Bayesian adaptive randomization based on interim efficacy data, and the ability to seamlessly add or remove treatment arms. Adapted to burn care, this would involve establishing a large-scale, multi-center clinical trial platform for burn management. The platform would initially randomize patients with major burns to receive either standard care or one of several distinct chitosan dressing formulations. Key outcome measures (e.g., time to re-epithelialization, infection rate) would be monitored continuously. Using adaptive algorithms, patient randomization would be weighted toward the dressing variants showing the most promise, while underperforming variants could be dropped for futility. This design maximizes efficiency and patient benefit, accelerating the identification of the optimal chitosan product for specific burn wound environments, much like oncology platforms match therapies to tumor biomarkers.
